> Regional Power: Maratha
Timeline:
Foundation -> 1674 by Chattrapati Shivaji Maharaj

Shivaji -> Shambaji(1680-89) -> Rajaram (89-1700) -> Tarabai {regent of ShivajiII} (1700-07) -> Sahu {son of shambaji} (released from Mughal captivity) -> Confrontation b/n Tarabai & Sahu
Ashtapradhan System:
 * It was initiated by Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj in 1679.
   
   Peshwa
   Prime Minsiter
   Majumdar
   Finance Minister
   Waqia Navis
   Interior Minister
   Shuranavis
   Secretary
   Sumant
   Correspondence Minister
   Sar-i-Naubat
   Commander in Chief
   Nyayadheesh
   Cheif Justice
   Pandit Rao
   Religious Affair Minister

 * In 1713, Balaji Vishwanath solved the dispute b/n Tarabai and sahu and he also acquired new powers for himself as the next peshawa.
   

Balaji Vishwanath (1713-20)

 * With his diplomatic tactics, he turned the office of peshwa into hereditary position.
   
 * He became the administrative head of the maratha empire and Chhatrapati Sahu as the figure head of empire.
   
 * He secured the mughal order securing Chauth and sardeshmukhi of Deccan to marathas.
   
 * He consolidated maratha confideracy of Saranjami.
   * Saranjami System
     * It is derived form the word "saranjam" which means temporary land grant.
     * It was initiated by Chattrapati Rajaram to seek the loyality of important maratha chiefs. in the return of land grant maratha chiefs provided military support to maratha empire.
     * Balaji Vishwanath turned the temporary land grant into a permanent land grant i.e. Watan
     * It led to the emergence of 5 important maratha houses
       
       Gaekwad
       Baroda
       Peshwa
       Puna
       Scindia
       Gwalior
       Holkar
       Indore
       Bhonsale
       Nagpur
 * Balaji Vishwanath assisted sayyiad brothers in the removal of Farrukhsiyar.

Peshwa Bajirao I (1720-40)
 * He was the greatest exponent of guerrilla warfare after Shivaji.
 * He was often referred as "Fighting Peshwa". In his entire military career of 20 years he never faced any defeat. After some point of time he gave a statement
   
   > Lets strike on the withering tree (Mughals) and the branches will fall on by themselves (Hyd, awadh, bengal). Lets establish Hindu Pad Pad Sahi (Hindu kingdom everywhere)

 * He defeated Nizam of Hyd and as per the t.o.Bhopal, Malwa became part of Maratha Empire.
 * He also assisted bundelkhand ruler Chatrasal against the attack of Md. Bangash. As a token of gratitude Raja Chatrasal offered hand of his daughter Mastani to him. Bajirao's family was monogamous by tradition. As such mastani was not very much accepted in the family.
 * It was Bajirao who picked up Scindia's and Holker's to play vital role in Maratha politics. Later, Schindia's and Holker's were responsible for shaping the direction of Maratha Politics.
   

Nanasahib/Balaji Bajirao (from Kashi bai) <- <- Bajirao I -> -> Krishna/Shamsher Bahadur(from Mastani)

Peshwa Balaji Bajirao (1740-61)

 * He became the next peshwa with the title Balaji Bajirao. He was regarded as the most powerful peshwa. Maratha territory reached its climax during his tenure.
 * It is said his territory extended from Cuttack to Attock(Afghanistan Border).
 * After the death of Chattrapati as per Sangola Agreement, all the powers of Chattrapati was transfered to peshwa and became De'facto ruler of Marathas.
 * In between 1745-51, Marathas raided Bengal and Odisha many a times. As a result bengal ruler Aliwardi Khan signed an agreement with marathas whereby he gave Odisha to marathas and also promised 1.25 lakh rupees annual payment to marathas.
 * After 1755, Bajirao forces ran through Punjab and expelled agents of Abdaili from punjab and occupied Lahore. It became major bone of contention b/n Abdaili and Maratha which led to 3rd B.0.Panipat in 1761.
   

1st Battle of Panipat (1526) -> Babur v/s Ibrahim Lodi
2nd Battle of Panipat (1556) -> Bairam Khan(akbar's general) v/s Hemchandra
3rd Battle of Panipat (1761)

Q: Why so many battles here?


# 3rd Battle of Panipat (1761):

 * Marathas were militarily most powerful people in india. They had the capacity to create an all India empire. But hardly there was any intent. There rule hardly went beyond
    collection of Chauth and Sardeshmukhi. It was only in the areas of Gujarat, Malwa and Khandesh, where they kept some system of administration but not in the areas of Punjab and Odisha. As such areas without administration were prone to attack by the others.
   
 * In 1761, attack of Abdaili delt severe blow to maratha pride. Maratha forces were led by Sadashiv Rao Bhau and his important military leaders included Vishwas Rao and Shamsher Bahadur. OTOH, Abdaili got the support of Rohilla's and Awadh ruler Shujaudaulah.
   
 * Abdaili brought Swivel guns in this battle which were operated from camels.
 * He also followed an intelligent strategy whereby he destroyed the road which connected panipat to delhi. Because of which not only military supply but also food supply was hit. It is said that there was near starvation in the Maratha Camp.
 * Maratha forces were thoroughly defeated and also led to death of Sadashiv Rao Bhau,
    Vishwash Rao, and Shamsher Bahadur.
 * Total 50k casualities happned in this war out of which most of them were from Maratha side. When the news of defeat was given to Peshwa he also died because of the shock.
   

It is said that panipat battle did not decided that who would rule India rather it decided who would NOT. Which means nor Maratha not abdaili will rule India. This way it paved the rise of BEIC, who consolidated its roots in upcomming decades.


Peshwa Madhav Rao (1761-72)

 * Marathas were highly indistrious people and they started working towards restoration of Maratha fortune. It was all possible due to able guidence of him, Nana Fadnis and Mahadji Scindia.
 * At this time the system of 12 administrator popularly called as Barah Bhai also started. Majorty of decision were take with the consultation of 12 administrators.

After the death of Madhav Rao, Narayan Rao (1772-73) became the next peshawa but unfortunately he was assassinated by his uncle Raghunath Rao.
 * It was 1st time in Maratha history that Maratha Peshawa was killed by his own blood.
 * Raghunath Rao remained as peshwa till 1774 and he was removed form power by 12 administrators which led to appointment of minor son of Narayan Rao i.e. Sawai Madhav Rao (1774-1795) as next Peshawa under the regency of Nana Phadnis.
   

-> Frustrated Raghunath fled to Surat where he signed a sectet treaty with BEIC. As per this treaty he promised area of Salsette, Bassein, and Bharuch to B's in return of position of Peshawa.

1st Anglo-Maratha War (1775-82)
 * When Nana Fadnis granted a port to french on the western coast, B's replied by sending their forces to Puna. Initially, NF wanted to avoid war and persuaded B's for T.o.Purandhar (1776). It was a short lived treaty which was not accepted by the Bombay unit of the company.
 * In this situation, Marathas led by Mahadji Scindia who consequtively defeated B's in B.o.Talegaon and B.o.Wadagaon
 * In 1780, Bombay unit of the company requested to GGofBengal Warren Hastings to guide B's. As such WH entered into war and defeated Mahadji Scindia near Yamuna river.
 * By 1782, this war proved to be inconclusive and ended with T.o.Salbai.
   * -> As per the treaty, Salsette and Bharuch was restored to BEIC and Bassein to Maratha.
   * -> Marathas assued company that in war against Mysore they will support B's.
   * -> British recognised sovereigh claim of mahadji scindia around gwalior so as north west of river yamuna.
   * -> B's agreed to pension of their protege Raghunath Rao.
     

In the very 1st war both the sides tested each others strength and t.o.Salbai resulted into 20 years of relative peace b/n the two powers. On one hand in this 20 years Maratha politics was going to disintegrate Otoh B's gained power to dominate Marathas.

Due to high-handed attitude of Nana Fadnis, Peshawa Sawai Madhav Rao commited suicide in 1795. After his death, son of Raghunath Rao, Bajirao II was appointed as next peshawa, under the regency of Nana Fadnis. Bajirao wanted to get reed to control of nana fadnis.

In 1800 natural death of Nana Fasdnis happened. After which, maratha sardars were divided on the question of support to peshawa. On one hand scindia's supported peshawa while Holkers refused to support. At this time peshawa performed cold-blodded murder of Vithaoji Rao Holker. In this situation, younger brother Yashwant Rao Holker decided to take revenge.

He attacked on Puna after which the terrified Peshawa fled to Bombay and signed t.o.Bassein or Subsidiary Alliance with B's.

Subsidiary Alliance System:
It was formulated by GG of bengal Wellesely to expand b's territory in India and to estd. British paramountey. {B's would be supreme power in India and Indian powers would be subordinate to B's}
 * The native power entering into alliance will have to accept b's forces in their territory and they will also have to pay the maintenance cost of British army.
 * If they failed to make the payments on time, in this situation some part of their territory will be chipped off as equivalent to payment.
 * Native power cannot enter in alliance with anybody w/o the permission of B's.
 * They cannot declare war against any other power without the permission of B's.
 * They cannot employ any European other than British in their court.
 * A British resident will be posted in the native court who will look after terms and conditions of alliance (but the British resident often Interfere in internal politics)
 * In return British promised that they will protect and safeguard native territory form any external or internal danger. {The promise which was seldom observed}
   

2nd Anglo-Maratha War (1803-05)

 * Scindia, Holker, Bhonsle, Gaikwads did not accepted the subsidiary alliance and they turned against peshawa and they decided to fight against B's. A very brave fight was put up by Scindia's against B's. But unfortunately they were consequtively defeated at B.o.Assaye and B.o.Lashwari. They were forced by B's to accept subsidiary alliance system.
   
 * OTOH, Holkers were able to harass B's forces at many places. In 1805, wellesely was recalled to Britain and he was blamed of unnecessary spending and fight in India when there is rise of Napoleon in Europe.
   

3rd Anglo-Maratha War (1817-19)

 * By 1817, Peshawa Bajirao was repentant that B's have captured Maratha Sovereignty and Marathas has lost their pride. He made the last bid to gain Maratha pride and independence. He tried to rally all the important Maratha sardars with him but it is only Holkers who came forward.
   
 * Like the previous time, once again, Maratha secured the support of Pindari's (informal Mercinaries whose loyalism was Money). In this last battle, Marathas were thoroughly defeated and so were Pindaris.
   
 * B's abolished the house of Peshawa and annexed Peshawas dominion into company's territory.
   

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> Regional Power: Modern Sikh state of Punjab

The land of Punjab was highly fertile area and also birthplace of non-sectrian religion Sikhism. Starting from Guru Nanak and by the time of Guru Gobind, Sikhism became the most popular and accepted religion of punjab. After the death of last guru in 1707, Banda Bahadur was the 1st political appointee of Punjab. After execution of Banda BAhadur fragmentation of Punjab started to happen. It led to creation of 13 confederacy know as Misl.

Out of these 13 confederacy -> 12 Sikh and 1 Muslim

By 1790's Sukerchakiya was the most powerful misl under the leadership of Ranjeet Singh. He tried to bring all the Sikh misl's together and by 1799 he was completely successful in the creation of modern Sikh state of Punjab.

[api/attachments/91QyFiqNFuxa/image/image.jpeg]


Maharaja Ranjeet Singh went on to create 2nd strongest army of Asia with the help of British officers like Ventura, Allard, Court and Gardner. He also introduced western methods of army training. He established very cordial relationship with B's.

In 1809, Maharaja decided to extend his territories in the cic-satluj area. At this time the local chiefs of this area appealed the B's for intervention. As such t.o.Amritsar was signed b/n B's and Ranjeet singh. As per the treaty maharaja recognized cis Satluj as British protectorate and in return B's recognized sovereign authority of maharaja in the trans satluj area.
The terms of treaty were respected till the lifetime of Maharaja. After the death in 1839, there was quick succession of rulers and at this time B's got opportunity to interfare in Punjab politics.
Within 2 years in quick succession following people were appointed as Maharaja:
Khadak -> Navnihal -> Sher -> Dhuleep singh (minor, Regent: Rani Jindan)
At the time of Dhuleep singh Rani Jindan was exercising power. At this time British intervention in punjab politics increased further. They also tried to create dissension in punjab court. They often provoked Khalsa army.

In 1845, on the pretext of building a bridge on satluj they brought huge army. It was a highly provocative act which led to 1st Anglo-sikh war in 1845.

In this fight Khalsa army was throughly defeated and it ended with t.o.Lahore. As per the treaty half of the punjab was taken over by B's and size of the khalsa army was also reduced to half. Moreover, Rani Jindan was supposed to pay 1 crore rupees as war damage price to B's. When punjab was unable to make the payment, B's sold kashmir to Gulab singh(Dogra Dynasty) in 75 lakh rupees.

British resident of Punjab removed governor of Multan (Mulraj) in 1848. He appointed his own agent in Multan. At this time mulraj revolted and this revolt was joined by Khalsa army. they made a last attempt to throw B's form punjab.

But in B.o.Chillianwallah, punjab army was thoroughly defeated and in 1849 B's completely annexed Punjab in company's dominion.

title: Administration of BEIC in India
content:
# Administration before 1773
 * The highest decision making body of the company was 24 Court of Directors. They were appointed by shareholders of company or court of properietor's on annual basis.
 * Cod created many committees to look after each and every aspect of the company.
 * In India company created 3 presidencies i.e. Bombay, Bengal and Madras. Each of the presidencies were absolute government in themselves, which means they were completely autonomous. Independent of each other and did not needed consultation with each other. They were only liable to report to 24 CoD.
 * In each of presidency, a president/Governor was the highest position. GG in Council was the highest decision making body of presidency. Each and every decision of a presidency was taken with the Majority vote of GG in Council.

# Regulating Act of 1773

 Background:
 * After the t.o.Allahabad, dual system of government became operational in bengal. As per the system Diwani -> B's and Nizamat -> British controlled bengal Nawab. This led to double harassment of people of bengal. Apart from it, in 1770, a major famine happened in bengal which swiped near about 1/3rd population of bengal.
 * At this point of time, people expected relief from British authorities but it was not provided. Moreover there was very heavy corruption in the company. Company officials were amassing huge amount of corrupt wealth for themselves.
 * At this time British parliament appointed a secret committee to look into the cases of corruption. The committee reported that company officials had aggregated so much of corrupt wealth that they can be better called as "Nawab's of London".
 * At this time British parliament did not wanted to be mute spectator when the people of bengal in hapless situation and reeling under the pressure.
 * In 1772, company applied for 1 million pound loan in the British parliament as the company was on the verge of bankruptcy {due to corruption}. The British crown represented by parliament grabbed this opportunity to check the accounts of the company and regulate its affairs.
   

    Provisions:
 1. G of B -> GG of B. Governors of Madras and Bombay were made subservient to him, which means they needed to report and consult with GG of B.
 2. To assist GGofB, GG Executive council was made with total 5 members. All the decisions were taken with majority
     vote.
    GG + 4 members

 3. It banned private trade by company officials.
 4. Every company employee on his return home needed to give a detailed statement regarding the wealth and property he has acquired and how he has acquired in India.
 5. Company officials were prohibited in taking any present or gift from the natives of India.
 6. It called for establishment of Supreme Court at Calcutta. Accordingly, SC was estd in 1774 with 1 Chief Justice and 3 other Judges. Elizah Impey was the 1st CJ of SC.
    

    Analysis:
 1. No where in the act any welfare measure for the people of Bengal was mentioned.
 2. GG was rendered powerless in his own council as very often the 4 Executive members ganged together to block the decisions of GG.
 3. Corruption in company and private trade continued as it is. Since there was no mechanism to stop pvt trade.
 4. Often there was clash b/n the judicial powers of GG and SC CJ. {overlapping jurisdiction}
 5. Bombay and Madras presidency were still acting on their own discretion and hardly they consulted to Bengal GG.
    

# Amending Act of 1781 -> Place the Judicial powers of GG above the Judicial powers of CJ of SC.

# Pitts India Act (1784):
It was named after British PM William Pitt Younger. This act tried to correct the issues of Regulating Act.
 1. It created a new body in Britain k/as Board of Control (BoC) with 6 members. British cabinet minister Secretary of state was made president of BoC.
 2. BoC was supposed to supervise the functioning of CoD. It led the beginning of Joint rule of British Crown and EIC in India.
 3. It differentiated the commercial and political functions of the company. {like commercial account and political account were separated so that functions of company can be monitored effectively}
 4. For the 1st time company's territory in India was referred as British Possession in India.
 5. One Executive member was removed from GGEC so as if GG gets support of 1 Ex. member then with his casting vote he can go ahead with his decision.
    GG + 3 members

 6. One of the provision of this act is that it prohibited interference in the native politics of the Kingdoms.
    

    Analysis:
 * A nebulous connection of corruption developed b/n BoC and CoD.
 * No check on pvt trade was effected by this time.
 * Even after removing 1 executive member, GG was still powerless in his council.

-> NOTE: In 1785, Cornvallis was appointed as new GG, he demanded overriding power in his council. As per the special act of British parliament, cornvallis was given over riding power in his council.

# Charter Act of 1793:

 1. It renewed the company's charter for next 20 years.
 2. The over riding power given to Cornvallis was further extend to all the GG's.
 3. British felt that Indian administration must be based on the written laws instead of personal rule of the rulers.
 4. It further called for translation and publication of written laws in the vernacular languages of India so that people can know about their rights and immunities.
 5. It also maintained that salaries of GG and his GGEC will be paid from Indian revenues and not from British exchequer.
    

# Charter Act of 1813:

    Background:
 * By the end of 18th century, Rise of Napoleon happened in France and Europe. He was arch rival of Britain. He created a continental system with which a large number of European countries got associated.
 * In 1806 and 1807 -> He released the Decree of Berlin and Milan respectively. Whereby he banned any kind of trade with Britain.
 * By this time Britain was at the peak of Industrial Revolution and it was manufacturing hub of the world. With the declaration of Napoleon the entire European market became unavailable for british manufacturers. At this time these manufacturers started to create pressure on British parliament to end the Monopoly of the BEIC in India and China.
 * They demanded access to these markets. They also quoted Adam smith's theory of "Laissez Faire" or “Free Trade Theory”

    Provision:
 1. It renewed the company's charter but company's monopoly of trade in India ended.
 2. However, company retained its monopoly in tea trade and trade with China.
 3. It clearly declared that the intention of British authority in India is to establish British paramountcy in India whereby British power would be paramount and supreme and Indian powers would be subordinate to B's.
 4. It allowed christian missionaries to settle in India under a license and they were entitled to promote moral and religious education in India.
 5. It allocated 1 lakh rupees from Indian revenue for the purpose of education in India. {However this money was not made available completely till 1833. In 1820s little bit of money was released as per Instrument of Instruction}

# Charter Act of 1833

 1. It renewed the company's charter for next 20 years but it completely ended the company's monopoly of trade in India and china.
 2. The Company was asked to wind up it commercial operations as soon as possible. However, the company was allowed to retain its political territory and administer it, but it was held in the name of her Majesty.
 3. It formally legalized colonization of India by Europeans.
 4. It added one Law member or Legislative expert in GGEC. His work was entirely rule making and he was not entitled as the permanent member of the council {no right to vote} -> Macaulay was the 1st law member of the council.
 5. It called for creation of 1st law commission to codify Indian Penal code civil and criminal procedure code. Accordingly 1st law commission was created under the chairmanship of Macaulay.
    * Section 87 of the act provided for education and employment of Indians. It further maintained that there would be no discrimination on the basis of religion, PoB, caste, race in the matters of employment.
 6. It designated GGofB -> GGofIndia; William Bentick was the 1st GG of India. He was the most powerful GG till this date. All the political and financial power were given to GG and GGEC. He can repeal/alter/ammend any law or regulation. His actions can only be disapproved by Cod who were acting under the supervision of BOC.
 7. It created the local government of Agra by separating for the Bengal.
 8. It called for upliftment of slaves in India.
 9. It further said while drafting any law rules related to Indian marriage and the rights of heads of families must be taken into consideration.

 NOTE: It recommended that Hailebury College in Britain must create a scheme of Quota to include future Indian civil servants. but this suggestion was disregarded.

    Importance of Charter Act of 1833:

 * It was the watershed movement in the Indian history. It led to centralization of Indian Administration, which further led to political and administrative unification.
 * This act made it clear that company is loosing its relevance in India
 * for the 1st time it was mentioned taht Indians would be included in the administration without any discrimination.
 * For the first time separation of work was recognized. for eg: Law expert for legislation.

# Charter Act of 1853:

 1. It renewed the charter of the company but for the first time did not fix any time limit.
 2. It created a separate GG Legislative Council with 6 new members.
 3. It started the system of Local representation in GG Legislative's Council. Out of the 6 new members 4 were to be added from the local governments of Bombay, Madras, Bengal, and Agra. The condition was nominated members must have completed minimum 10 years in the service of BEIC.
 4. It reduced the number of CoD from 24 to 18. Out of which 6 were to be nominated by the British crown.
 5. It called for recruitment of Indians in Covenanted Civil Services. In this regard Macaulay committee was constituted to create the scheme and syllabus of exam.

-> Satendranath Tagore was the 1st Indian to clear this exam in 1864.

> Pillars of BEIC's Administration

# Civil Services
The word "civil" was used to denote the revenue services of the company. It was used to differentiate military aspect from revenue aspect.
 * Cornvallis is Regarderd as creator of Modern Civil services in India. When he came to India, he observed company's civil servants have become highly corrupt. These civil servants were appointed by CoD's which often led to nepotism and favoritism. Moreover private trade was also important reason for corruption
 * On one hand effectively banned pvt trade and on the other hand he also raised the salaries of Indian Civil servants and they became the Highest paid civil servants in the world.
 * He also ensured promotions are happening on the basis of seniority.
 * Cornvallis was highly prejudiced against Indians and he made a statement that
   
   > All the natives of Hindustan are corrupt

 * He led to Europeanization of Civil services. In 1793, he laid an official rule , that all the higher post in administration worth 500 pound annual salary will be held only by the British.
 * It was followed probably because of the following reasons:
   * There was high demand and popularity of Indian Civil Services among the B's in Britain.
   * Cornvallis thought that Indians dont fit in the British scheme and they will not serve the British interest will full integrity.
   * He was highly convinced that British colonial interest
      will be better served by British only.
 * Cornvallis also relieved the district collector from his judicial functions and now he was enterest only for the revenue administration.
 * In 1798, when wellesely came to India he decided to create a civil servant training college at Fort William Calcutta. But later his actions were disapproved by court of Directors and they created a separate civil servant training college at Hailebury in England.

# British Army

There were two branches of company army in India: the Queens Army and Company's Troops
Queens Army

Company's Troops

It was the small branch of the British army and it was an army of occupancy. It only included British. It was the ultimate symbol of British hold over India.
It was army of expansion who fought battles in many areas. It was the biggest branch of Company's army which mainly included Indians as Sepoy and British as officers.

By the time of the 1857 revolt in the company's army 86% Indians were there. It is very surprising that how handful of Britishers were able to control such a large Indian nature army. Followng reasons can be cited for it.
 * At that time there was no spirit of pan-india nationalism.
 * It was a good opportunity for employment for Indians as british recruited from all the sections of society.
 * British were good pay masters as such Indians served as good mercenaries.
 * Indians had the tradition of loyalty towards salt, which they kept till their relation was organic with british.
 * All the higher positions were kept for british so that British can maintain effective control over army.

# Police Services
In the pre british India law and order was maintained in Ad-hoc manner. There were many officers who were responsible for law and order function.Eg:
 * At village level -> Chowkidar, Fauzdar, and Amils
 * Zamindari Area -> zamindars were responsible for law and order function. Zamindars maintained a troop of Thanedars for the purpose.

Cornvallis noted that very often that these thanedars gave free hand to Dakaits who looted the villages towns and they shared the loot with zamindar. He revealed zamindar form law and order function and decided to create police organization for the purpose.
 * He divided districts into many circles k/as Thana. At thana one Indian person was appointed as in-charge k/as Daroga.
 * At the district level he created the office of Superintendent of Police

Later in 1833, William Bentick abolished office of SP and gave the charges of SP to district collector.
Qn: 18th century India was beret with spectre of fragmented polity. Discuss how B's got benefits from it.
-> Imperialism: Extension of rule from one country to another country. It happens through the way/tool of colonialism.
-> Colonialism: Acquired territory and then dominating polity, Sociey and economy.

# Judicial Organization under British
Warren Hastings created british judiciary in line with mughal model. He created
 * Diwani Adalat -> Civil cases
 * Fauzdari Adalat -> Criminal cases

He also enlisted the help of hindu and musllim law experts like Qazi's and Mufti's. For appeals of civil cases Sadar Diwani and for criminal cases, Sadar Nizamat Adalat was created.

Later cornvallis brought many changes in the existing judicial system.
 * Now diwani Adalat was designated as district Adalat. and Fauzdari adalat was abolished.
 * At the next level he created 4 provincial courts of circuit and appeal at Patna, Dhaka, Calcutta, andd Murshidabad.
 * Cornvallis also created gradation of courts in India which is as follows:
   

[api/attachments/9EhiJwI0r9Kv/image/image.jpeg]

When William Bentick came to power he abolished 4 provincial courts and at its place he created Judicial commissionaries. Once again the charges of the judicial commissionaries given to civil servants/district collectors.


LAND REVENUE POLICY


# Permanent Settlement

 * It is also c/as Zamindari Bandobast. It was initiated by Cornvallis in 1793 in the area of Bengal Bihar in the beginning but later it was extended to Odisha, Northern districts of Madras and Varanasi.
 * It covered near about 19% of area of British India.
 * The settlement was b/n british authority and zamindar that's why it was named Zamindari Bandobast. Since the revenue was permanently fixed it was also called permanent settlement.
 * From the permanently collected fixed amount 10/11th was the share of B's and 1/11 for the zamindar.
 * There was no fixation of rates which peasants will be paying to zamindars.
 * Land ownership right was given to zamindar and it was hereditary and transferable. But the ultimate ownership of the land lay in the hand of british authority.
 * One crucial part of the settlement was the Sunset Clause. If zamindar is not able to make the payment on time it will lead to confiscation of his landed state and his zamindari is also snatched.
 * In case of crop failure due to any natural calamity, No relief was provided.

    Reasons for its implementation:
 1. Cornvallis wanted a permanently fixed amount for long period of time.
 2. He anticipated that in the longer run when the tax rates are not increasing, the settlement would be beneficial to the peasants and they will have more saving to invest in land fertility.
 3. Cornvallis did not wanted to invest the british energy in the hard task of revenue collection but he wanted to focus on expansion of british regime.
 4. They wanted to create a paradigm shift in Indian society whereby B's will be creating a new class of zamindar who will be friendly but also loyal to british raj.
    

     Impact of settlement:
 * British:
   * It fulfilled the targeted objectives of B's to get a permanent amount for a longer period of time. but later B's were repentant that they must have kept the provision of revision of land revenue rates in future (It was inherent in the law that B's cannot Increase revenue rates even if they wish to).
     
 * Zamindars:
   * A good number of zamindars lost their land ownership right due to sunset clause. But majority of the zamindars reaped huge benefits.
   * It also led to the beginning of Subfeudalization in Indian society and rise of large number of intermediaries and Absentee landlords.
     
 * Peasants:
   * It led to economic decline of peasantry of bengal. Their situation became worse than hand to mouth existence. There was pressure on them to pay the revenue on time for which they started to take loans form moneylenders on exorbitant interest rates.
   * As a result many of them were caught in vicious cycle of debt. It further led to increase in peasant suicide but also increased instances of revolt against british authority and zamindars.
     
 * Land:
   * One of the biggest casualty of the settlement was the land fertility of bengal. B's were not at all concerned about investment in land and they left it to zamindars. but zamindars were more concerned about increasing their profits.
   * Peasants were not
      the owners of land and they were not in a good economic situation to support the fertility of land. As such the agricultural land of bengal started to loose its fertility.
     

# Ryotwari Settlement

 * In 1792, two british officers, Thomas Munro and Captain Reed implemented this settlement in the area of Baramahal in Karnataka as an experimental project. But it took time for the settlement to be implemented on a broader level.
 * In 1820, Thomas Munro implemented in Madras presidency and Elphinston implemented it in Bombay presidency.
 * It covered approx 51% area of the British India.

    Provision:
 1. The settlement was concluded b/n B's and peasants i.e. Ryots that's why it was named Ryotwari.
 2. Land ownership right was given to the peasants it was hereditary and transferable. But the ultimate ownership of land lay in the hands of British authority.
 3. If the peasants defaulted on the taxes it led to confiscation of their land and snatching of their land ownership right.
 4. The settlement necessitated the establishment of a big revenue department for the collection of revenue. As such a large number of people were recruited for the purpose.
 5. It provided relied to the peasants in case of crop failure due to any natural calamity.
 6. They kept a provision of revision in land revenue rates after an interval of 30 years.
    calculation and imposition of taxes a comparative scientific method was adopted. Whereby the total expense on growing a particular crop was to be deducted from the total worth of the crop and on the remaining amount taxes were to be imposed.
 7. It was further said that the maximum rate of taxes would be 50% and it must not exceed 50%.
    

    Reasons for implementation:
 * They wanted to promote cultivation of cash crops in India. So that raw material can be sent to Britain to feed their industries.
 * As per the utilitarian thought, land ownership was given to the peasants so that he develops emotional attachment to land and he may work extra to keep the fertility of land intact.
 * It was also thought that if something if left in the hands of peasants then only he will be interested in cultivating the cash crop.
 * It was a learning from permanent settlement that they must keep scope of revision in land revenue rates.
 * It was a good measure to keep direct control over the peasantry over India.

    Impact of this settlement:

 * British:
   * It fulfilled all the targeted objectives of british authority like supply of raw material to british industries, a good sustainable income and direct control on Indian peasantry. But it also increased the administrative cost of revenue collection.
   * It led to Commercialization of Agriculture which means cash crops were promoted instead of food grains. Which led to scarcity of food grains and further inflation in food grain prices.
     
 * Peasants:
   * The impact of settlement on peasants was no different than the other settlement. The tax burden was high and in case of crop failure they were not given any relief.
   * Though the settlement did not include intermediaries on paper but in the process intermediaries got their positions.
   * When the big revenue department estd. village elites like Poligara and Mirasidars comfortably placed themselves in the revenue ranks. They became agents of british authority. After getting their appointments they started to use insider information for their own good. they started to purchase irrigated lands on cheaper rates and they also resorted to torture and extortion.
   * In 1855, Madras Torture Commission was created. It warned British authority if the grievances of Peasents is not addressed then it will implode into a bigger revolt.
     

# Mahalwari Settlement
 * It was introduced by Holt Mckenzi in 1819 in the western UP. Later by 1822, Martin Bird extended it to rest of India. It covered near about 30% area of british India.

Provisions:
 1. It had mixture of both permanent and Ryotwari system.
 2. For the collection of revenue, Mahal as fiscal unit was chosen. Mahal was group of villages.
 3. Revenue collection rights/agreements were signed with village headman, Thikadar's, Lambardar's.
 4. Land ownership right was given to the peasants. It was hereditary and transferable. But the ultimate ownership of land lay in the hand of British authority.
 5. From the collected revenue, british share was 2/3rd and rest was for the revenue collectors.
 6. Tax rate on peasants varied on the basis of land holding. Taxes were calculated after the land survey and measurement and land record was also maintained.
 7. There was provision of revision in revenue rates after an interval of 30 years.

     Reasons for its implementation:
 * As per utilitarian thought land ownership was given to the peasants.
 * B's wanted to encourage cash crops like sugarcane and cotton in this area to feed the British industries.
 * To save administrative expense in collection of revenue, contracts were signed with intermediaries.
 * Learining from ryotwari they also kept provision for revision in revenue rates after an interval of 30 years.

Impact of settlement:
 * All the boxes for British authority were rightly ticked in this settlement and they reaped huge benefits so as saved the administrative cost of collection.
 * It also benefited the intermediaries
 * It led to commodification of land which means land became a commodity to be easily sold and Purchase in the land market. {B's authority facilitated the creation of land market in which rate of the land was decided by them}
 * Land survey was at the core of the settlement that it failed miserably. Faulty land records were created on the basis of idiosyncretic estimate but not on the basis of actual measurement.
 * The agricultural depression of 1829 further aggravated the problems of peasants of north India. Their anger and frustration was to be expressed rather loudly in the revolt of 1857.
path: Modern Indian History / Administration of BEIC in India

title: Civil and Tribal Uprising
content:
# FEATURES OF THE TRIBAL SOCIETY

 * Tribal society are homogeneous in nature, which means they all belong to the same genetic code or they all are blood related.
 * Tribal society's were unstratified in nature. which means there were no division in their society.
 * They generally inhabited geographically isolated areas and they never liked interference of outsiders in their area.
 * They practice unique culture whereby nature was given very high importance.
 * In some of the tribal society's sacred Groves were highly revered whereby they protected some elements of nature.
 * There was balance sexual division of labor in society's.
 * They had distinct means of livelihood which was dependent on nature.
 * Tribal practiced common property holding pattern and they didn't practice the concept of pvt property.
   

# EASTERN INDIA & BENGAL

 1.  Sanyasi Uprising (1770):
     * In bengal large number of Naga and Giri Sanyasi's were present. They were known for visiting holy religious places and they often carried religious weapons with them.
     * In 1770, b's imposed restriction on their movement and they were not allowed to visit any religious place. In this situation they started attacking british factories and Treasuries.
       
 2.  Fakir Uprising (1776):
     * Fakirs were wandering Muslim religious mendicants. They also provided religious services by visiting one village to another. They also carried weapons while travelling.
     * British authorities imposed restrictions on their movement also.
     * → In this situation Fakir and Sanyasi of Bengal started to collude with each other and combinedly attacked on British treasuries.
     * Chiraj Ali, Bhawani Pathak, and Devi Chaudharani were the notable supporters of Sanyasi & Fakir Uprising.
     * Later a novel Anandmath was written by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, which was based on the background of these revolts. One of the unique aspect of this novel was the song "Vande Mataram". Which later became song of the freedom struggle.
       
 3.  Pagal Panthi Uprising (1830):
     * It was a semi religious sect founded in north bengal by Karam Shah. Later in 1830, his son Tipu took up the cause of peasants against exploitation by zamindars and british agents.
     * He advised peasants to withhold the revenue and not submit before the exploitative people.
       
 4.  Khasi Uprising (1829):
     * British were creating a road to connect Brahmaputra valley with Sylhet.
     * For this purpose large number of labors from plain areas were brougt but later after the completion of road, B's tried to habitat the outsiders in this area. In this situation Khasis organized the revolt under the leadership of Tirath Singh.
     * Very soon this revolt was joined by the other Tribal communities like Garo, Khampti's and Singhpo's.
       
 5.  Kol Mutiny (1831):
     * Kol were the tribal of Ranchi, Singhbhum, Hazaribagh and Palamu. It is mineral belt of India and Britishers tried to control this area through overt and covert measures.
     * they started transferring land from Kol headman to outsiders from bengal and other plain areas.
     * In this scenario, Kol organized revolt under the leadership of Buddhu Bhagat. They tried to burn property papers of transfer. B's suppressed this revolt with immense brutality, which led to killing of >1000 Kol People.
       
 6.  Paika Rebellion (1817):
     * Paikas were the landed militia of the kingdom of Khurdha(OD). They provided military support to Khurdha king and in return they were granted rent free states.
     * In 1803, B's started to tinker with the land revenue system of Odisha, which often led to peasant uprising.
     * In 1817, B's revoked rent free estates of Paika's and now they were supposed to make payments. In this situation, Paika's organized revolt under the leadership of Bakshi Jaganandhu.
     * Initially they were successful of expelling british forces form khurdha. Later B's came with greater prep and suppressed the revolt.
       
 7.  Ahom Rebellion ():
     * During the 1st Burma War (1824-26), B's used upper Assam area or Ahom territory. They pledged that after the end of war they will withdraw form Ahom territory but it was a false promise.
     * In this situation, ahoms organized revolt under Gomdhar Konwar. It was a successful rebellion which led to handing over Ahom territory to their Raja Purandhar Singh Narendra.
       
 8.  Khond Uprising (1838-56):
     * Khond's were the primitive tribes of Niamgiri Hills of Odisha. They were known for their inhumane practice of Human sacrifice once in a while {Mariah}.
     * B's tried to stop this initially through persuasion but later through force, when a rebellion was organized by Chakra Bisoi.
       
 9.  Fairazi Revolt (1837-56):
     
     * It was a semi religious sect in Islam founded by Haji Shariat-ullah. His son Dadu Mian, took up the cause of peasants and asked them to withhold the revenue and do not pay any taxes to the B's and Zamindars.
       
     * He gave a statement "All the land belongs to the god, and no man has the right to levy taxes on it"
       
 10. Santhal Revolt** (1855-56):
     
     * It was the most massive of all the tribal revolts. In santhali language it is called as "Santhal Hool".
       
     * Santhals were the tribes of the area b/n Bhagalpur and Rajmahal, which they called Daman-i-Koh. They cleared the dense forest of the foot hills of rajmahal and made it agriculturally cultivable.
       
     * B's brought santhal area under umbrella of many taxes and also started to bring large number of outsiders particularly form bengal in this area. It led to livelihood crisis and also destruction of unique culture of santhals.
       
     * Very soon santhals figured out that B's were behind it and they were hand in glove with outsiders(Santhali -> Diku).
       
     * In this situation two brothers, Sidu Murmu and Kanhu Murmu emerged as their leaders. They maintained that Thakur has communicated to them to take up arms against diku's and it will bring satyug.
       
     * But unfortunately this uprising was suppressed with immense brutality.
       
     * B's claimed killing of 15k santhals but in reality more than 25k people were killed.
       
     * One of the impact of this revolt is that in later times British declared Santhal area as a separate paragna with more autonomy given to santhal community in their administration.
       
     * Charles Dickens on Santhal people -
       He was highly impressed by their sense of battlefield ethics, going so far as to contrast their honor favorably against European armies of the time.
       
     * > "There seems also to be a sentiment of honour among them; for it is said that they use poisoned arrows in hunting, but never against their foes. If this be the case and we hear nothing of the poisoned arrows in the recent conflicts, they are infinitely more respectable than our civilized enemy, the Russians, who would most likely consider such forbearance as foolish, and declare that is not war."
 11. Munda Revolt (1899-1900):
     
     * This movement was initiated by Birsa Munda. Mundas were the tribal people of Jharkhand particularly Khunti, Tamar, Bandagaon, and Ranchi.
       
     * Their main resentment was against British authorities and outsiders who were involved in land grabbing. Initially Birsa Munda tried to fight it through the route of Judiciary, seeing no results through the process, he decided to organize a big revolt against the exploitative system of British rule.
       
     * He envisaged a egalitarian society which was free from any kind of exploitation, internal or external.
       
     * During the course of the revolt he was arrested and tried in court but unfortunately due to poisoning in jail his death happened.
       

# North India

 1. Wahabi movement (1840s):
    
    * It was religious revivalist movement in Islam based on the teachings of Abdul Wahab of Saudi Arabia.
      
    * Initially they were against the Sikh rule of Maharaja Ranjit Singh but after annexation of Punjab by B's, they turned against british and in 1857 revolt they participated in large numbers.
      
 2. Namdahari or Kuka Revolt
    
    * It was a religious purification campaign in Sikhism initiated by Bhagat Jawahar Mal in 1840.
      
    * But later after the annexation of Punjab they took up arms against british authority and participated in large numbers in 1857 revolt.
      

# West India

 1. Bhil Uprising (1822 - 1846):
    
    * Bhils were the tribals of Khadesh area of Western Ghats. They were not reconciled to british rule in their area. Further british occupation to their area brought agricultural hardships, employment hardships, restriction on forest movement and burden of taxes.
      
    * Initially revolt was organized under the leadership of Seva ram
      
    * By the end of 1846 there was agreement with B's whereby B's allowed access to forest produce to Bhil community.
      
 2. Koli Uprising (1829-1844):
    
    * They were the fisherman community of western ghats. Earlier they had good source of living through fishing activity.
      
    * But B's brought livelihood crisis for them and also destroyed the coastal forts of Koli's.
      
 3. Ramosi Uprising (1822, 1825, 1835):
    
    * They were the hill tribes of western ghats and they mainly resided in higher altitudes. They had complete control on the vegetation and produce of higher altitudes. British authorizes imposed restriction on their usage of forest produce.
      
    * Initially they revolted under the leadership of Chittur Singh but later in 1838 when British removed their Raja Pratap Singh of Satara, the entire community took up arms against British authority.
      
 4. Gadkari Revolt of Kolhapur and Sawantwadi (1844):
    
    * Gadkari's were the traditional military class of Maratha empire. They protected the Maratha force. But after British occupation of this area, gadkari unit was disbanded facing employment crisis initially they revolted form Kolhapur and later form Sawantwadi.
      

# South India

 1. Revolt of Raja of Vizianagram (1783-94) -> Northern Circar area:
    
    * In northern Circar area of Andhara, B's demanded 3 lakh rupees for the recognition of his rule of Raja of Vizianagram. He refused which led to b.o.Padmanabham. But unfortunately he was killed and later his son reconciled with B's by paying 5 lakh rupees.
      
 2. Revolt of Dewan Velu Thampi (1805) -> Travancore in Kerela:
    
    * Velu Thampi was the dewan of Travancore kingdom. Travancore was put under subsidiary alliance system and after some time they realized it is difficult to rule due to high handedness of B's. He was assisted by Nair community of Kerela.
      
    * But unfortunately velu thampi was captured and hanged publicly to instil fear among people.
      
 3. Rampa Rebellion (1879) -> exactly same as Ramosi:
    
    * Rampa's were the hill tribesman of eastern ghats and they inhabited higher altitude of hill ranges. British authority imposed restriction on their forest movement and use of forest produce.
      
    * Facing livelihood crisis, they revolted which led to the recognition of their forest rights.

title: 1857 Revolt
content:
# CAUSES

It was the most massive of all the revolts. It was a landmark event in the Indian history which led to end of an old era and beginning of new era. It was the final culmination of 100 year long tradition of fearce resistance against british authorities. The causes of the revolt were intertwined together and it is difficult to determine the sequence of the revolt.

The circumstances prevailing in the 1857 did not occurred in the past and not tended to reoccur in the future. The underlying causes are as follows.

 1. Immediate Cause of revolt
    
    * British authority replaced old Brown Bess Musket with new enfield rifles for their greater range and accuracy. In the new rifle catridge was quoted with a paper and paper was smeared with cow and pig fat.
    * Before loading the catridge in rifle, paper was to be biten off. It hurted the religious sentiments of Hindua and Muslim sepoys alike.
    * When they refused to greased catridges, they were fired form the services.
    * Later they were also handed over hard punishments, which fuelled the revolt.
      
 2. Military causes of the revolt.
    * Enlistment Order of 1856
      * As per this order sepoys were supposed to serve where ever they are ordered to go i.e. even in the other british colonies.
      * It led to doubt in the mind of sepoys, that in foreign land they will
         be forced to embrace Christianity. Some of them were driven by superstitious belief that crossing the sea is a sin and they might loose membership of their community.
    * Earlier sepoys enjoyed free postage privilege but after the Post office act of 1850s this privilege was withdrawn.
    * Whenever a Sepoy served far from the home location he was given bhatta or allowances but after 1850 it was withdrawn.
    * An Indian sepoy in the army was paid 1/3rd the salary as compare to European sepoy. It was not a problem till additional privileges were given but after the withdrawal of privileges they started to feel relative deprivation.
    * There were increased instances of racial discrimination in the army and many a time sepoys were mistreated with the boots.
    * Majority of the recruits in the army were from bengal, awadh and north west frontier province. where caste and class consolidation was very high. Whenever upper caste Hindus and upper Muslims were humiliated before the lower class recruits, the upper caste and class felt that it was deliberate attempt of B's to break the traditional social fabric of India.
      
 3. Economic Causes of the revolt
    * Exploitative Land revenue systems
      * Under all the land settlements be it Permanent, ryotwari or mahalwari. there was heavy burden on peasants to pay the revenue on time. All of them were highly exploitative. All the revenue settlements led to peasants getting caught in vicious cycle of debt, increase in farmer suicide rate, commercialization of agriculture, commodification of land etc.
        
    * Despoilation of Indian Trade, Handicraft and artisan products
      * Right from the beginning of 18th century, b's enjoyed one way free trade but on the other hand Indian traders had to pay high amount of taxes when they are selling their products outside India.
      * The manufactured goods of Britain despoiled the Indian handicraft and artisan products.
        
    * Confiscation of rent free states.
      * In 1850, govt constitutes "Inam commission" to look into the title deeds of rent free estates.
      * Till 1856, 21000 cases were brought before commission. It ordered confiscation of all 21k rent free estates.
      * the people who were mostly affected with this confiscation were Maulvi's and Ullema's who gave religious services. When the revolt started they participated in large numbers.
        
 4. Socio-religious causes of the revolt
    * Right form the beginning, british policy was identified with certain degree of reform in Indian society. but the way these reforms were imposed, it created ambivalence towards old orthodox order in the society. People did not liked top-down approach of social reform {legal way to do this} and they also questioned that
       b's have no rights to interfere in the social affairs of Indian society.
    * B's often disregarded rituals and customs of Hindu and Muslim society. They often called them as Barbaric and Bigots.
    * The Missionary activities and their forced religious conversions further created furore in society.
    * in 1850, government came up with hindu religious disability act which tried to modify many of the rituals and customs of Hindus. It created doubts in minds of hindu society that they will be forced to embrace christianity.
      
 5. Political causes of revolt
    * The subsidiary alliance system of wellesely eroded the trust b/n B's and indian princely states. Indian rulers realised that they gave false promise of protection and in the guise of it they chipped off indian territory for payment.
    * In 1848, GG Dalhousie came up with Doctrine of Lapse. As per policy if an indian ruler dies without a successor form direct blood then adoptions from the extended families will not be accepted and the native territories annexed into british indian territory. With the application of it following princely states were annexed: Satara(1848) -> Jaitapura -> sambhal -> Bhagat -> Chota Nagpur -> Jhansi -> Nagpur (1854). The annexation of these territories was done with utmost disrespect towards the native rulers. This wholesale swiping away of Indian territories were not liked by the subjects of these areas and once the revolt started they participated in huge numbers.
      

# COURSE OF THE REVOLT:

 * The very first instance of refusal of use of greased catridges happened in Berhampur area.
 * In Feb 1857, 19th native Bengal infantry, refused to use the due rifles after which they were disbanded from the army.
 * In March 1857, Native infantry of Barrackpore, Calcutta refused to use the new rifle. One of the sepoy Mangal Pandey went a step ahead and killed his commanding officer. He was overpowered and executed.
 * The most important event happened on 23rd April 1857 in Meerut, where native cavalry refused to use the greased catridges. They were tried by a court martial and judgment was pronounced on 9th May. Uniforms of sepoys were stripped off and they were sentenced 10 years hard imprisonment. Moreover they were fettered and marched to jail.
 * Next morning i.e. 10th may, other sepoy revolted and released the sepoys in Jail. By the evening they killed all the British officers in Meerut and marched towards Delhi.
 * 11th May they reached delhi and proclaimed mutiny in the name of Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Jafar.
 * Very soon revolt spread to entire north, western and eastern India. South India remained lesser affected with the revolt.
   

# PROMINENT PERSONALITIES:

 * Delhi: the symbolic leadership was with mughal emperor Bahadur Shah but the original command lay in the hands of general Bakht Khan who brought his troops from Barelley.
 * Bareilly: the former ruler of Rohilkhand Khan Bahadur took charge in bareilly.
 * Kanpur: Original command was in the hand of Nana Sahib (aka Raja Bithoor), he was son of last peshwa Bajirao II. Tantya Tope was his most trusted aid. Nana Sahib was enraged with discontinuation of his family pension.
   * When the mutiny started he joined it. But in the later stages he fled to Nepal
 * Awadh/Oudh: Nawab Wajid Ali Shah of Lucknow was deported to Calcutta by british authority. After his deportation, Begum Hazrat mahal organised administration of Lucknow as the regent of her minor son Bijris Qadir.
   * She organied administration with the help of both hindu and muslim official but B's started to interfare in the affairs of Lucknow.
   * When the mutiny started BHM also joined the rank of mutineer's.
 * Faizabad-Ayodhya:
   * the revolt was led by Maulvi Ahmadulla who was originally from Madras, but long back shifted to Faizabad.
   * He was offended by confiscation of his rent-free estate. He was one of the outstanding leaders of revolt.
 * Bihar:
   * Revolt from Bihar was organized by zamindaar of Jagdishpur Kunwar Singh. He was a man in 70s but regarded as one of the most outstanding leaders of the revolt. His zamindari estate was confiscated by B's as per sunsset clause.
 * Jhanshi:
   * Original name of Laxmi Bai was Manikarnika. She was born in a Marathi family at Benaras. Her father was a respected officer in peshwa Bajirao II court. A part of her upbringing happened in peshawas court. She was trained in many martial arts like a warrior. She got married to Raja Gangadhar Rao Navalkar of Jhansi who gave her the title of Laxmi Bai.
   * After the death of raja their adopted son was not recognized by B's and they annexed Jhanshi by application of DoL
   * Driven out of Jhanshi, rani gave a battle cry of "Mai apni Jhansi nahi Dungi". She got the support of Tantya Tope and fought bravely with the britihsers. She martyred during the combat. After the suppression of revolt, British General Hugh Rose paid homage to Rani by saying “...the Indian mutiny has produced but one MAN and that man is a WOMAN...”

In the course of revolt Tantya Tope was captured and hanged by B's. Maulvi Ahmadulla was killed by a pro british raja of Shahjahanpore. Kunwar Singh died in the battlefield during the combat.


# REASONS FOR THE FAILURE OF THE REVOLT:

 1. There was absence of feeling of pan India nationalism at this time.
 2. The ideology behind the revolt did not prove to be effective. The idea of violent overthrow of british rule could only happen when there is a central leadership and common ideology.
 3. There wasn't any effective communication but most of the time primitive mode of communication was used.
 4. The revolt happened in an Ad-hoc fashion. and it was not happening in a seamless way.
 5. Revolt was parochial in outlook whereby everyone was fighting for their own self interest.
 6. Revolt didn't spread to southern India in a way it was expected.
 7. Many of the rulers and prominent zamindars became collaborator with the B's and supported suppression of revolt.
 8. The intelligentsia did not supported the revolt as they felt it was backward looking trying to re-establish the old feudal order
 9. OTOH, B's operated under a central command, effective organization and a very clear communication. The commander in chief of British forces was regularly informed about rebel activities through telegraph services. B's were also laces with sophisticated weapons.
    

# SIGNIFICANCE OF THE REVOLT:

 * There was complete cooperation b/n H's and M's during the course of revolt.
 * It led to complete transfer of power form BEIC to British Crown.
 * Even in its failure its served great purpose by implanting the seed of nationalism which further facilitated the creation of many regional organizations and finally leading to the creation of an All India organization to fight for indians.
 * British authority became cautious after the suppression of revolt and started to indians in many administrative roles.

title: Socio-Religious Reform Movements
content:
In 1828, RRR wrote “The present system of religion adhered to by the hindus is not well calculated to promote their political interest”
Max Weber observed “Hinduism had become compound of magic, animism and superstition and abominable rites like animal sacrifice and physical torture has replaced the place of worship”
Reform Movement
Revivalist Movement
The proponents of reform advocated change in certain aspects of religion, they went on to say If any practice which is not justified on the parameters of rationality, equality, human dignity must be removed from the religion.
They felt, the present form of religion has been diluted due to the western influence. They advocated return back to past practices which are pure and undisturbed.
They highly emphasized on rational values and often maintained that western education is full of rational values.
they opposed reformist and maintained that rationality is not the core preserve of the west. They further said greatest of the rational values can be found in the Indian textual traditions like Upanishad.
They often enlisted help of British authority to come up with social reform with legal route.
They never enlisted help of british authority and they were highly anti-imperial in nature. They always advocated overthrow of british rule from India.
They enjoyed a very narrow social base
They enjoyed a wider social base
They followed Top-down approach
They followed Bottom to up approach


19th Century Reform
20th century reform
The agents of change majorly belonged to the upper section of society. and subjects of change were majorly backwards and women.
eg: RRR, ICV, KCS
Both agents of change and subjects of change majorly belonged to backward section of society
eg: Periyar, Narayan Guru, Dr. Ambedkar
Agenda of change was not very broad and it was limited to the themes like education of women and backwards, upliftment of untouchables, widow remarriage, ban on sati and child marriage.
Agenda of reform was very broader. They demanded equal political right, citizenship, economic right, Rtp, RtV, access to public facility etc.
They enjoyed narrow social base
They enjoyed a wider social base

# REFORMS IN HINDUISM

 1.  Brahmo Samaj:
     * Raja Ram Mohan Roy:
       * He was in the writership of the company and he had expertise in more than 10 languages.
       * He believed in the scientific approach, values of rationality, human dignity and equality.
       * He believed in Monotheism. To prove his point he translated many Upanishads. He deeply studied all the religions in a comparative manner. After it he wrote a famous text "Tuhfat-ul-Muwahiddin". English version of this was "Gift to Monotheist". Another popular text he wrote "Percept of Jesus".
       * In 1815, he founded "Atmiya Sabha" for the purpose of social reform in Hinduism. He also released reform magazine in Bengali language i.e. "Sambad Kamudi". In this magazine he regularly wrote about inhumane practices like Sati.
       * Due to his persuasion, B's banned Sati Pratha in 1829.
       * In 1828, RRR founded Brahmo Sabha which was soon renamed as "Brahmo Samaj". This organization had two fold objective: 1. Worship and adoration of Eternal, unsearchable and immutable God. 2. Promotion of benevolence and charity in society.
         
     * Debendranath Tagore:
       * He founded Tattabodhini Sabha in 1839. He was blend of India-western thought. He was deeply rooted in Indian tradition but also acknowledged importance of western education.
       * In 1842, he joined Brahmo Samaj and infused a new life in it. He started to work on two fronts
         1. Carry forward reform agenda of BS in Hinduism.
         2. To criticize proselytizing of Christian missionaries.
            
     * Keshub Chandra Sen:
       * He joined BS in 1858. He was known for his very good oratory skills. And he inspired a great lot of Indians to initiate social reform. He called for respect towards all the religions and also to bring th e teachings of all the religions at one place. (cosmpolitinization)
       * He started to promote intercaste marriages and called for to ban child marriage. He was very critical to orthodox Hindu rituals and rigidity of caste system. Due to his radical thought process he was expelled form BS in 1865. he went on to create BS of India in 1866.
       * In 1878 he performed the marriage of his minor daughter to the minor prince of Cooch-Behar, with all the orthodox Hindu rituals. It led to division in his cadre and majority of his followers left him and went on to create "Sadharan Brahmo Samaj".
         
 2.  Young Bengal Movement:
     * It was initiated by an Anglo-Indian Henry Vivian Derozion, he was professor in Hindu college in Bengal. He was highly inspired from French revolution
     * He tried to inculcate the values of French revolution like liberty, equality and fraternity to his disciples. he asked his disciples to think freely and oppose all the authorities with relevant questions.
     * He also inspired them to oppose all the decadent customs in India.
     * In a short span of time he gained a large number of followers who were k/as Derozian's. Because of his thought process he was expelled from Hindu college but soon after this incident he fell ill and died at a very young age. His Death led to complete halt of this movement.
       
 3.  Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar:
     * Because of his immense knowledge in Sanskrit language he was given the title of Vidyanagar. He was also one of the most renowned name in the sphere of Bengali literature. It was he who refined the way prose is written in today's time.
     * He is often regarded as “Father of Bengali Prose”.
     * He dedicated his life for the emancipation and upliftment of the women. He served as secretary the famous Bethune school, which was pioneer of girl's education in Bengal.
     * He also served as principal of Sanskrit College in Bengal.
     * He persuaded British authority for widow remarriage reform as such B's came up with Hindu widow remarriage act 1856.
     
     * He also released a religious reform magazine in Bengali language i.e. “Shome Prakash”.
       
       
 4.  Ramakrishna Mission & Swami Vivekananda:
     * This organization was named after Ramakrishna Paramhansh who was priest at Kali Temple at Dakshineswar {banks of Hooghly, Calcutta}. 
       He had immense sympathy for human suffering.
     * He always maintained “Service to mankind is service to Godkind”. He gave a mantra “Yatra Jiva Tatra Shiva”. He also up help the utility of idol worship in evoking spiritual response.
     * Narendranath Dutta was his favorite disciple. Right form his childhood Narendranath was deeply interested in the study of Hinduism. He deeply studied Hinduism but couldn't find solace until he found Ramakrishna Paramhansh.
     * Under the guidance of his guru his spiritual started to happen.
     * In 1893, he participated in All World religion conference held at Chicago, USA. He gave a very profound speech in this conference and immerged as modern preacher of Hinduism. He said “Motion is the law of life and that stagnation results into degeneration”, He further added “There is a need to strike the balance b/n Materialism of the west and spiritualism of the east. Western world can learn spiritual processes from India and eastern world and in return they can give access to Material progress of the west to east".
     * He advocated the social reforms as popularize by Bengal social reformers eg. removal of superstitious discriminatory practices, education for all, widow remarriage, no child marriage etc. He did not involve himself in any political activity of the time. But he inspired the Indian youth. He infused sense of glory in India's past and immense faith in India's future.
     
     * To carry forward his idea of social reform and to build a nation he founded Ramakrishna Mission in 1897 at Belur.
       
       
 5.  Arya Samaj:
     * It was a revivalist movement, initiated by a Hindu ascetic Dayanand Sarswati. Initially organization was created at Bombay but soon it was transferred to Lahore (1875).
     * He was very critical to influence of priestly class and the Purana literature. He believed that it has led to dilution of Hinduism and original Hinduism existed in the Vedas. He gave a mantra “Go back to Vedas”.
     * He also believed that western influence and forceful conversions in the medieval ages also led to dilution. In this regard he initiated “Shuddhi Movement” (This was Proselytizing Movement). It was an attempt to bring those people in the fold of Hinduism who had converted to Christianity and Islam in the previous ages.
     * He also called “India for Indians” and wanted overthrow of British rule.
     * On of the contribution of Arya Samaj was Publication and popularization of Vedic literature. They released 3 popular magazines i.e. “Ved Bhashya”, “Ved Bhashya Bhumika”, and “Satyartha Prakash”.
     * Progressive Arya samajist like Swami Hansraj went on to create Dayanand Anglo Vedic School at Lahore (1886) where both traditional education and English medium scientific education was also imparted.
     * Conservative Arya Samajist like Swami Shraddhananad went on to create Gurukul Pathsala in Haridwar in 1901 where education was imparted in traditional Sanskrit medium.
     
     * {Bhagat Singh was also from DAV School}
       
       
 6.  Theosophical Society of India: {Theosophy → “Godly Wisdom”}
     * It was founded by Madam Blavatsky and Colonel HS Olcot in 1875 at USA. By 1880 they shifted to India and created HQ of Theosophical society at Adyar Chennai.
     * Within few years they went on to create more than 100 branches of Theosophy. They developed immense faith in the divine wisdom mentioned in the Upanishads. They also developed faith in concept of Soul, rebirth, Karma, Re-incarnation and trans-migration of Soul, Nirvana, Kaivalya and Moksha.
     * Theosophy got big impetus when Ms. Annie Besant joined it in 1893. Under her guidance theosophy was also colored with nationalist spirit.
     * She observed “the need of India among others is the development of nationalist spirit based on Indian ideals and education, it can be enriched by the thought and culture of the west but it must not be dominated by west”.
     
     * In 1898, She went on to create Central Hindu College at Banaras where both traditional Hindu education and western sciences were taught. Under her guidance theosophy conducted many researches on Indian Scriptures and also they were translated and published in many languages.
       
       
 7.  Prarthana Samaj (1867):
     * It was founded by Dr. Atmaram Pandurang in 1867 in Bombay. He was highly inspired from the speeches of KCS.
     * Mahadev Govind Ranade was the most famous leader of this org. He wrote a very famous article “A Theist confession of Faith”. In which he emphasized on worship and adoration of god similar to the one mentioned in the writings of Maratha Bhakti Saints (God Vitthal or Vithobha of Pandharpur).
     * They also beleived in Monotheism. As far as their social reform agenda is concern it is similar to brahmo samaj like women education, upliftment of backwards and their education, promoting rational values, WR, and ban on child marriage.
     
     * MGR also went on to create an organization of political "Poona Sarvajanik Sabha". He was also one of the initial founder of INC.
       
       
 8.  Truth Seeker Society / Satyasodhak Samaj:
     * This organization was founded by Jyotiba Phule in 1875. With this organization he wanted to break the dominance of upper caste in society. He believed that all the problem of untouchability emerges form caste system. He advocated for abolition of untouchability caste system must be abolished. Through this org we wanted upliftment of women's and backwards so as to ensure their education.
     * He thought his wife Savitribai Phule and sister of his friend Fatima Begum. These two ladies went on to become the 1st female teachers of Modern India. They founded the girls school at Bhidewala Puna.
     * JP also created an orphanage.
     
     * His most popular reform magazines were “Ghulamgiri” and “Sarvajanik Satyadharma”.
       
       
 9.  Pandita Ramabai:
     * She was famous social reformer from Puna area. She founded Arya Mahila samaj (1881) for the education of girl child and women. She also founded Mukti mission to help the women who are in distress situation or are abused by their family (particularly widows). She created Sharda Sadan to provide refuge to women and disables and also impart them vocational training so that they can earn their livelihood.
     
     * She emerged as biggest supporter of Rakhmabai in case against Dadaji bhikaji. This case was related to restitution of conjugal rights. Due to intervention of ramabai it led to historic judgement of Queen Victoria which further led to passing of age of consent act 1891.
       
       
 10. Kandkuri Veersalingam:
     * Perhaps he was the 1st social reformer from Telugu speaking area and often regarded as father figure of Telugu Renaissance.
     * He was inspired by the speeches of KCS and motivated by it, he went on to create a girls school at Rajamundry. He also carried forwarded the reform agenda of Brahmo Samaj and also created a reform organization “Hitkarini Sabha”.
     * He also initiated a profound movement against Practice of Dowry.
     
     * He also contributed towards refinement of modern Telugu literature.
       
       
 11. Shree Narayan Dharma Paripalana (SNDP) Yogam:
     * This movement was initiated by Shree Narayan Guru in Kerala in 1901. He belonged to Ezhava Caste of Kerala who constituted 26% of Kerala.
     * Traditionally ezhavas were involved in toddy tapping. Narayan Guru tried to instill values of hygiene in them and initiated a very profound movement for their equality.
     * He demanded political representation of Untouchables, RtE, Economic rights, access to public facility and temple entry.
     
     * He gave a mantra “One caste, One God and One Religion”. Later his followers changed it to “No caste, No god and No religion”.
       
       
 12. South Indian Liberal Federation / Justice Party / Dravida Kazhagam:
     * It was the 1st anti upper caste movement form Madras Presidency. It was founded by TM Nair and P Thyagaraja in 1916.
     * They talked about removal of superstitious orthodox practices, upliftment of backwards and untouchables and sociopolitical recognition of backwards. In 1920s, SIL Federation enters in to provincial elections with the name Justice party.
     * Later it came under the influence of Self Respect Movement of E V Ramaswamy Naicker aka Periyar. Under his influence Justice party withdrew form electoral politics and made this org a social reform org Dravida Kazhagam (Dravidian Federation).
     * Later under the leadership of Anna Durai it became Dravida Munetra Kazhagam and started to participate in electoral politics. After the India's independence DMK happened to be the 1st party which demanded caste based reservation in India.

# REFORMS IN ISLAM:

 1. Aligarh Movement:
    * it was initiated by Sir Sayyiad Ahmad Khan of Aligarh. He was a loyalist member of the Judicial services of the Government. He retired in 1876 and became the member of Imperial Legislative Council in 1878.
    * Earlier he wrote a famous article “Asbab-i-Bagawate-Hind” → “causes of revolt of 1857”. In this article he rejected the notion of 1857 revolt as Muslim conspiracy against British authority. He said it was the aggressive expansionist policy of BEIC which was the major reason behind the revolt.
    * He suggested to the B's that if they are serious about avoiding any future clash like 1857 then they must include more Muslims in Administration.
    * He stood for modern western education of Women and so as the entire Muslim community. To realize the dream of modern western education he created Mohammadan Anglo oriental college at Aligarh in 1875.
    * Later in 1920, this college went on to become AMU.
    * On the end of social reform he was against the practice of polygamy, purah system, and Piri-Muridi (Blind Followership).
    * He wrote a popular reform magazine “Tahzib-ul-Akhlaq” → “improvement in manners and morals”.
    * In 1888 he was conferred with the title of knighthood by british government. he pledged his loyalty towards British rule as such the Deo-band Organization issued a Fatwah against him.
 2. Darul-uloom-Deoband (1866) {→ against Sir Saiyyad Ahmad Khan}:
    * It was founded by Rashid-Ahmad Gangohi and Qasim Nanotavi in 1886 at Saharanpur in UP. It was a revivalist organization, which believed that original practices of Islam has been diluted due to the external influence like British rule.
    * They had two objectives:
      * To revive the original teachings which are mentioned in the Quaran, The Hadith, the sunnah.
      * To initiate spirit of Jihad against British authority and throw out British rule out of India.
    * Jihad means → Ability to differentiate b/n right & wrong and take the side of right. Right from the beginning it was a nationalist organization and it welcomed the foundation of INC.
 3. Ahmadiya Movt {→ inspired from Brahmo Samaj}:
    * they are highly persecuted sect in Islam. In 1879, Mirza Gulam Mohammad emerged as Ahmadiya leader and he proclaimed himself as the awaited Messiah or Mahdi.
    * He called for respect towards all the religions and he was against spirit of jihad against anyone.
    * He stood for Modern Western education of Ahmadiya community.
    * His efforts paid and large numbers of ahmadiyas were able to move out of poverty and their status was uplifted due to modern western education.

# REFORMS IN SIKHISM:

 1. Nirankari Movt:
    * Word “Nirankar” → “Shapeless / formless God”. It was initiated by Baba Dayal Das, who was contemporary of Maharaja Ranjit Singh.
    * BDD was of critical MRS's excessive spendings. He was also critical to idol worship, tomb and grave worship.
    * He also proposed simple version of marriage or “Anand Karaj” that is to be performed in front of the holy text Guru Granth Sahib.
 2. Kuka or Namdhari Movt:
    * I t was initiated by Bhagat Jahawar Mal in 1840s to bring the reforms in orthodox practices of Sikhism. It became a very profound social reform movement under Baba Ram Singh.
    * BRS was very critical to entry of caste system in Sikhism and he also started a very strong movt against female infanticide.
 3. Singh Sabha's:
