    * It was collective body of reform in Sikhism. They were very critical to conversion activities of Christian missionaries, Muslims and hindus.
    * their most important objective was to bring benefits of western education in Sikh society. For this purpose 1st Khalsa college was created at Amritsar in 1892.
 4. Akali Movement (1920s):
    * Akali movement was against corrupt mahant's who were controlling Sikh gurudwaras. These mahants were agents of British authority and they were diluting the original tenets of Sikhism.
    * It was completely peaceful and non-violent movt. They were successful in creating pressure in B's which led to passing of Shiromani Gurudwara Prabandhak Committee Act 1922.
    * It was implemented in 1925.

> Note: At this movement Gandhiji congratulated Akali's that 1st phase of freedom struggle has been won by them.

# REFORM IN PARSI RELIGION (ZOROASTRIANISM):

It is one of the oldest religion in the world. Though it emerged in Iran, but later Indian became home of Parsi's. Its a rich community but also known for extreme ritual and practices.

Eg. They don't bury or cremated their dead instead they leave their dead bodies fallow in open space called as Silence Tower. They also maintain purity of blood and don't accept any conversion.

People like DBN, Fardoonji Naranji, and SS Bengali started the change in Parsi society.

They created an organisation of reform Rehnumai Mazadyasan Sabha.

DBN released a reform magazine “Rash-Goftar”.
path: Modern Indian History / Socio-Religious Reform Movements

title: Struggle Begins !
content:
# FACTORS WHICH FAVORED RISE OF NATIONALISM:

 1.  Collision of the interests: Many of the Indian society members started to realize that their interest would never be served in their country till the time b's are ruling India. Just because the presence of B's, Indian intellengsia class skipped away form the administration, Indian trade and industry was completely despoiled and peasants were highly oppressed.
 2.  Administrative unification of India: British authority unified India administratively for ease of administration but it also led to unification of grievances of people. Now they started to identify B's as their common enemy and reason behind their grievances.
 3.  Development of Modern transport and communication:
     * Development of railways was done with the British perspective. But up to some extent it facilitated in the growth of nationalism. First railway line was laid in 1853 b/n Bombay to Thane. By 1900, 25000 miles of railway line was laid down. In 1865, a British officer Edwin Arnold made a prophecy “Railways may do for India what dynasties have never done, what the genius of Akbar could not achieve by his good governance not the cruelty of Tipu Sultan. Railways may make India truly a nation”.
     * In b/n 1848 and 1854, modern postal system {→ by Dalhousie} and telegraph system was initiated which also facilitated in rise and growth of nationalism.
 4.  Inaugural of Western Education: This proved to be highly momentous for the rise and growth of nationalism. Now the renaissance literature of Europe was available in India and Indians were inspired by thought process and ideas of European thinkers like: John Locke, Voltaire, Montesquieu, Thomas Hobb, JJ Rousseau, Jeremy Bentham, JS Mill etc. Even ideas of French revolution like liberty, freedom and Equality also started to inspire Indians. Many of the Indians had first hand experience of working in Europe. once they returned back to India they found condition to be very slavish. They started to ask basic questions with British authority that what is the intention of British rule in India.
 5.  Growth of vernacular Press: The first newspaper of India was Bengal Gazette by James Augustus Hickey in 1780. After which many other newspapers in English language were also released. The growth of vernacular press in India picked up pace after revolt of 1857. Large number of vernacular newspapers like: Amrita Baazar Patrika {Ghosh Brothers}, Bengali {Girish Chandra Ghosh and Surendranath Banerjee}, Indu Prakash {Vishnu Parshuram Pandit}, The Hindu {G. Subramania Iyer, M. Veeraraghavachariar, and N. Subba Rao Pantulu}, Keshari & Mahratta {Bal Gangadhar Tilak}, Indian Mirror {Devendra Nath Tagore and Manmohan Ghosh}, Young India {Mahatma Gandhi}, New Weal (Commonweal) {Annie Besant} etc. These all newspapers started to publish exploitative aspect of British rule.
 6.  Rise of New Middle Class (Educated Middle Class): The new middle class of India was purely created on the basis of education. After getting their appointments in administration, they started to reflect that why India was colonized by handful of British. They introspected and realized that it is due to many rigid divisions in the society. They felt that to throw out British rule in India all the Indians will have to come together and forge a new identity of being Indian.
 7.  Inspiration from India's glorious past: Large number of European scholars came to India and they deeply studied Indian literature and upheld the superior wisdom mentioned in these texts. After discovery of IVC, British authority maintained that one of the most advanced civilization of the world existed in Indian subcontinent.
 8.  Inspiration from Revolutionary Activities in other parts of world:
     1. On the ruins of Spanish and Portuguese empire many nation states emerged in South America.
     2. The role of revolutionaries like Garibaldi and Mazzini was often quoted by Indian leaders as an inspiration.
     3. In 1905, small Japan defeated big Russia. These revolutionary activities broke the myth of invincibility of big powers.
 9.  Racial discrimination policy: British followed the policy of racial discrimination since the inception of their rule in India. For eg:
     * Though civil services was open to Indians but practically it was very difficult. The maximum age was reduced to 19 years by 1876.
     * Ilbert Bill Controversy: Viceroy Ripon introduced this bill which was aimed at ending Judicial Disqualification on the basis of racial distinction. in other words if bill gets passed Indian judges will be entitled to try European convicts also. But introduction of this bill led to huge protest in British parliament and finally it was modified from its original version.
     * → Now it became very clear to Indians that justice and fair play cannot be expected form B's where interest of their own community is also involved at the same point of time.
 10. Economic Critic of Colonialism:
     * One of the biggest contribution of early nationalist of India was economic critique of colonialism
     * DBN was the 1st and foremost personality who criticized british colonialism. The 2nd important was RC Dutta who wrote “Economic History of India”,
     * Other notable leaders included leaders like GKG, MGR, GSI, PC Ray, GV Joshi.
     * These leaders were able to trace colonization of indian economy in all its 3 layers i.e. Trade, Industry, and Finance.
       * Trade:
         * There was one way free trade for BEIC traders and their products were free of any customs duty but when indian products entered into any other market they were imposed with very high customs duty which made their products uncompetitive.
       * Industry:
         * Right form the inception of british rule, british policy was all about 
           supporting the british industries and ensure uninterrupted raw material supply to british industries form India.
         * As a result hardly any industry developed in India. And moreover if at all any industry was operating, it faced dearth of raw material.
       * Finance:
         * Financial imperialism is the worst stage of economic colonization.
         * In India it happened through development of railways.
         * The entire capital for railway development was brought from Britain and british investors demanded hefty security of investment as such it created vested interest which further perpetuated british colonialism in India.
     * Drain of Wealth:
       * Dada Bhai Naoroji was the high priest of theory of drain of wealth. He maintained that britain is not only draining India of its resources but also bleeding India.
       * British officer John Sullivan who was president of Board of Taxes Madras beautifully explained drain mechanism He said “Our system very much acts like a sponge, drawing uup all the good things form the banks of Ganges and squeezing them down to the bank of Thames”.
       * DBN explained 3 components of drain of wealth:
         1. Remittances - It was salaries and savings of british officials which they sent to Britain.
         2. Home Charges of Indian government in Britain - It included Pensions of british officials, interest on public debt and any expense of indian govt in Britain which was to be paid form indian revenue.
         3. Excess of export over import - for which India got no material return. It was mainly due to export of raw material and not the manufactured goods.
       * DBN further calculated the drain amount to more than half of the GDP of India, 1/3rd of total savings and more then total land revenue of India.
       * Drain of wealth theory an economic critic had a greater merit to be easily understood by the nation of peasants. No other thought can better provoke Indians then the thought that they are being taxed in India so that people living in far land can live in comfort.

# FOUNDATION OF CONGRESS:

After the suppression of 1857 revolt many regional organizations came up to create political awareness in certain parts of country the important regional organization were:

 1. Landholders’ Society (1837-38):
    * The founders were Dwarkanath Tagore and Radhakant Deb.
    * The objective was to safeguard the rights of zamindars.
 2. British Association of Bengal:
    * Founded by George Thomson in 1843.
    * Objective: Safeguarding the rights of the common public.
 3. British India Association:
    * The above two organizations were merged to create British India Association.
    * The objective is to safeguard the rights of the common public and zamindars.
 4. Bombay Association: (1852)
    * Founder: Dadabhai Naoroji.
    * It was created for demanding legislative assembly for Indians.
 5. East India Association (1866):
    * Created in London. By Dadabhai Naoroji.
    * It calculated the Drain of Wealth.
 6. East India Association of Calcutta: (1876)
    * Founder: Surendra Nath Banerjee and Anand Mohan Bose.
    * Purpose: To protest against the reactionary measures of Viceroy Lytton and demand political advancement of Indians.
 7. Madras Mahajan Sabha: (1884)
    * M V Raghavacheriar, P Charlu, G Subramaniyan Iyer
    * To increase the number of Indians in legislative assemblies.
 8. Bombay Presidency Association (1885)
    * Founders: Pherozshah Mehta, K T Telang, Badaruddin Tyabji.
    * To demand more political rights for India's, and to increase their numbers in legislative assemblies.

Foundation of INC:

 * By 1884, the leaders of all these organizations realized that they are not effective in their regional capacity and time has come to create an All India organization to create effective pressure on foreign rule.
 * As a result in 1885, INC was founded. Its creation was facilitated by a retired british civil servant Alan Octavian Hume.
 * 1st meeting of congress was convened at Gokuldas Tejapal Sanskrit college at Bombay on 28th Dec.
 * This session was presided by Womesh Chandera Banerjee.

Theory of Safety Valve and lightening conductor:

 * After the foundation of congress, many theories were proposed regarding its creation. The most popular was ToSV, which was backed by LLR. As per this theory, INC was created by AO Hume as a Safety Valve to British empire in India.
 * Prima Facie it was supposed to address the grievances of rising middle class in India but its original intention was to avoid any serious revolt like 1857 against B's. In a way it wanted to safeguard British empire so that British rule would continue for forever in India.
 * Leaders like GKG rejected it and maintained that congress was not the sole idea of AO Hume but also Indian nationalist. He further added that in the prevailing circumstances, congress is the best option to try for redressal of grievances of Indians.
 * He said while being in INC, Indian nationalist can act as lightning conductor for the freedom of India.

# 1ST PHASE OF FREEDOM STRUGGLE (1885 TO 1905) {AKA MODERATE PHASE}:

Soon after the creation of congress, Indian freedom struggle entered into its very 1st phase. Which is aka moderate phase of freedom struggle. The initial leaders of congress included names like DBN, RC Dutt, PC Ray, MG Ranade, GGK, GV Joshi, G Subramaniya Iyer, SNB, KT Telang, Pherozshah Mehta, 
Badruddin Taiyyabji, Anand Mohan Bose, P Anand Charly, V Raghava Cheriar.

These leaders adopted moderate method of struggle whereby, the believed in Prayers, Petitions, Memorials to British Authorities and Constitutional Agitation. They never believed in bypassing any law but to work within the four walls of law.

They didn't had faith in the capacity of masses and they never initiated any mass movement. They wanted to create political awareness in society and also to spread political education.

In the legislative councils they demanded Political and material advancements of Indians and they also demanded reforms in Civil Services, simultaneous conduct of Civil Service Exam in India and also increasing the number of Indians in the Civil service.

Their most important contribution was economic critic of colonialism

 * They were able to trace colonization of Indian economy in all its three layers i.e. Trade, Industry and Finance.
 * They also explained drain of wealth from India and how Britain was depriving Indians from their own resources.

# 2ND PHASE OF FREEDOM STRUGGLE (1905-1915)

→ aka Extremist Phase / Passive Resistance Phase.

On 19th July 1905, Viceroy Curzon announced partition of Bengal on communal line. He created East Bengal with Muslim majority and west bengal with Hindu majority population (earlier, he made a statement → “Bengal united is a strengthen and bengal divided will fall of in many ways”)

East bengal had areas like todays Bangladesh and assam. and west bengal included bengal, Odisha and Bihar.

The decision was implemented on the 16th of October 1905. With the partition of bengal, Indian freedom struggle entered into its 2nd phase which is called as extremist or passive resistance phase.

At this point of time some of the leaders of congress talked about going ahead with mass movement or passive resistance strategy {prayers and petitions must be backed by the pressure of passive resistance}.

These leaders were popularly called as extremist leaders of congress with names like BGT, LLR, Bipin Chandra Pal, Aurobindo Ghosh and VO Chidambaram Pillai. The greatest representative of this thought was Bal Gangadhar Tilak.

Bal Gangadhar Tilak

 * He was a Mathematics teacher at Fergusson College Pune.
 * BGT was the most famous extremist leader at this time. He joined congress in 1890s.
 * To increase his popularity he started to celebrate Ganpati Festival (1893) and Shivaji Festival (1895).
 * He also released two magazines i.e. Keshari (Marathi) and Mahratta (English).
 * In 1897, for the 1st time he was given Jail term of 18 months for writing provocative article in Keshari. When he was released from Jail, he popularized slogan “Swaraj is my birth right and i shall have it”. Originally it was coined by Francis Baptist (Kaka Baptiste).
 * It was Tilak who maintained that the Hindi language written in Devanagari script must be used as a National Language

The pressure created by extremist leader resulted into onset of passive resistance movement.

Annual session of INC 1905 (Banaras)

 * It was presided by GKG. In this session he approved beginning of Swadeshi and Boycott movement.
 * It said "Indians will boycott Manchester clothes and Liverpool salt".
 * Both Swadeshi and boycott were interlinked and based on the development of Atmashakti (Self-Reliance).

Swadeshi Movement:

 * Majority of the developments of Swadeshi Phase was in the field of culture and education which is as follows:
   * RNT wrote “Amar Sonar Bangle” which later became national anthem of Bangladesh.
   * Dakshinaranjan Mitra an emotional song “Thakurmar Jhui” which is even popular till this date.
   * Bankim Chandra Chatterjee's song “Vande Mataram” became song of freedom struggle.
   * Abanindranath Tagore broke the European tradition of painting and started to paint on the indian themes. His iconic painting was “Bharat Mata”. In this painting “Bharat Mata” was depicted holding a piece of cloth, bunch of paddy, rudraksha beads and a book. These items were indicative of basic necessities of indians and spiritual and secular knowledge.
   * [api/attachments/EwcPhNQvjJxL/image/image.png]
   * Abanindranath Tagore along with his Japanese friends went on to create “Oriental Society of Art” which started to offer scholarship to bright students. Nanadalal Bose was the 1st recipient of this scholarship and later on he went on to become the most famous painter of INC.
   * Near about 800-900 National schools were created a large number of bright students were sent to Japan and Germany to study engineering and technology.
   * In Maharashtra, Sakharam Ganesh Deushkar, wrote “Desher Katha” in 1904 in which he warned against "Colonial state Hypnotic Conquest of Mind". Later when partition of bengal happen this text became very famous and within few months >2000 copies were sold. Later in 1910, British banned this book.
   * Acharya PC Ray created "Bengal Chemical Factory" (Pharmaceutical)
   * VO Chidambaram Pillai created “Bharat Swadeshi Steam Navigation Company”.

Limitations of Swadeshi Boycott movement:

 * It was not able of garner the support of the muslim masses particularly in bengal mainly due to:
   1. Divide and rule policy followed by British authority particularly in bengal.
   2. Reactionary leadership among muslim masses in bengal.
      * Foundation of All India Muslim League - 1906:
        * It was founded by Nawab Sallimullah of Dhaka and Agha Khan and it was supported by then viceroy Minto.
        * On the day of Its foundation ML maintained that they created this platform to address the grievances of Muslim community. Their intention is not to create communal disharmony in society but only to promote muslim interest.
        * They also pledged their complete loyalty to British authority.

Annual Session of INC 1906 (Calcutta):

 * Before the session differences emerge b/n moderates and extremist leaders on the question of boycott. Extremist leaders wanted all out boycott of British manufactured goods and services. OTOH, Moderates wanted to stick to boycott of Manchester clothes and Liverpool salt.
 * Extremist leaders wanted their own president in Calcutta session but moderates leaders announced the name of DBN who was equally respected by both sides.
 * DBN tried to consoliate b/n the two groups and gave 5 resolutions:
   1. Swadeshi
   2. Boycott
   3. Swaraj (self rule in line with British colonies in Australia and Canada)
   4. National education
   5. Annulment of Partition of Bengal.
 * These resolutions were not able to solve the differences b/n extremist and moderate leaders.

Annual Session of INC 1907 and Surat Split:

 * Originally session was planned at Nagpur which was hub of extremist leaders but on a very short notice moderate leaders changed the venue of session to Surat. So that Tilak will not be able to contest elections.
 * {Aurobindo Ghosh brought 3000 congress people from Calcutta but Bombay being closer moderated called 7000 of their guys}
 * In this session moderate leader Rash Bihari Ghosh was elected as congress president.
 * {When Tilak was speaking, Rash bihari Ghosh did not allowed him to speak much, but tilak continued. Many moderate guys approached tilak with sticks. Fight started → mayhem in the assembly. A chappal was thrown to Rash bihari but he doged and it hit Pherozshah Mehta🤣 (he was sitting beside SNB) → Pherozshah Mehta took this to his heart. → did not allowed extremist to rejoin INC, in fact he was the biggest block → 1916 when he died → within a week both sides were united 🤣}
 * Unfortunately in this session leaders of both the factions Faught fiercely with each other.
 * Moderate leaders captured the entire machinery of congress and expelled the extremist leaders form the congress. This episode is famously k/as Surat split of congress.

# BRITISH ADMINISTRATION AFTER 1857:

Queen Victoria's Proclamation 1858:

 * On 1st Nov 1858, GG Canning read out Queen Victoria proclamation at a durbar held at Allahabad, which formally ended Mughal Rule and initiated rule of British crown.
 * Queen's proclamation disclaimed any intention of conquest in India.
 * It assured that rights of Indian Princely states would be respected.
 * It further said that British crown will follow the policy of Justice, benevolence and religious toleration.
 * It further added that customs traditions rights usages faith etc of indians will be respected and Indians will be entitled public service and education without discrimination on some grounds.

GoI Act 1858: → {This was in haste, Therefore was not elaborate}

 1. It ended the company's rule and so as the abolition of CoD and BoC.
 2. GG of India → also designated as Viceroy of India (as a viceroy he is representative of British crown and as a GG he is Administrative head of India)
 3. British cabinet minister Secretary of State was made responsible for GoI.
 4. To assist SoS a 15 member Council of India was created (later it became Imperial Legislative Council / Central Legislative Council)

Indian Councils Act 1861:

British authority realized after the suppression of 1857 revolt that it is very important to include indians in the process of rule making and as such it led to the beginning of representative institutions in which indians will also be the part.

 1. Indian members were included into central legislative council as non-official members but British retained official majority in the council.
 2. It also recommended creation of provincial legislative council as such it was created in Bengal (1862), NWFP (1866), and Punjab (1897).
 3. Viceroy was canning was entitled to initiate portfolio system where by he created different departments of administration.
 4. It recommended establishment of High courts as such 3 HC's were created at Bombay, Madras and Calcutta. The supreme court at Calcutta was subsumed within HC.
 5. GG was entitled to issue ordinances on important matters w/o the connivance of his council.

Indian Councils Act 1892:

 1. It increased the number of Indians (non-official members) in both central and provincial councils but official majority was retained in both of it.
 2. To fill some of non-official seats indirect election method was followed but it is to be noted that no where in the act word “election” was mentioned.
 3. Indian members were allowed to discuss budget and address a question to British.

Morley-Minto Reforms / Indian Council Act 1909 / GoI Act 1909:

 1. It decided to give majority to non-official members in provincial legislative council.
 2. But it retained official majority in central council.
 3. For the appointment of non-official members an electoral college was created the members of it were basically privileged and rich class of India eg. rich traders, merchants, local representatives, teachers, professors etc. Income was the criteria to be included in the electoral college.
 4. Indian members of the council were not only allowed to discuss the budget but also vote on separate budget items. They were also allowed to ask supplementary question to Executive.
 5. It announced Separate Electorate for Muslim Masses. Moreover the income criteria of muslim masses was kept way lower then the other communities.
    * As per the provision of separate electorate, in particular constituency candidates would be the muslims and so is the voters.
 6. One indian member was to be added into viceroy ex council and Satyendra Prasad Sinha was the 1st indian member.

Moderate leaders were highly dissatisfied with 1909 act. They expected Parliamentary form of responsible government in India but SoS Morley clearly mentioned that he has absolutely no intention of introducing Parliamentary form of government in India.

Indian leaders conveyed this dis-pleasure to higher British authorities in Britain. As such in 1911, Grand Delhi Durbar was held and for the very 1st time British monarch King George V visited India to participate in durbar.

He announced annulment of Partition of bengal but also the creation of two new stated i.e. Bihar and Odisha. It was also decided that capital of British India would be transferred form Calcutta → New Delhi.

Two British architect, Sir Edwin Lutyns and Herbert Backer were entrusted with the task of developing new architecture at Delhi.

# REVOLUTIONARY NATIONALISM PHASE -1 (1905-1915):

Pre-Revolutionary events (before 1905):

 1. Vasudev Balwant Phadke (1876-77)
    * often regarded as father of indian armed rebellion after the revolt of 1857.
    * Around 1876 he created a group of Ramosi's, Bhil's, Mahar's and Dhang's and marched towards pune. He expelled British authority from pune and captured its administration for a week.
    * Later he was arrested and tried in court.
    * He was defended by GV Joshi, but unfortunately 1883 his death happened due to hunger strike.
    * [api/attachments/TlIk57ixtoMI/image/image.png]
 2. Chapekar brothers (1897):
    * In 1890s there was severe spread of Bubonic Plague in Bombay presidency.
    * At the same point of time two British officers WC Rand and Lt. Ayerst were involved in many atrocities against people who were affected with plague.
    * At this time, motivated by the article of Tilak written in his magazine Keshari, Chapekar brothers killed these two British officers.
    * It was the 1st example of political assassination in India.
    * The wrath of this killing fell on Tilak and he was handed jail term of 18 months.

Reasons for rise of revolutionary nationalism:

 1. Partition of bengal on communal line was a trigger to initiate revolutionary nationalism.
 2. Youth of India never coincided with the methods of moderated leaders {efforts of Moderates did not result in any substantial change in the Indian political scenario} they considered moderate methods are like begging for their own rights in their own country.
 3. There was high dissatisfaction with other British administrative policies and also the tactics of divide and rule.
 4. Indiscriminate killing of Indians by the British of frivolous charges. Youth of India regarded extremist leaders as their role model. When Tilak was arrested and deported to Mandalay Jail youth became determined to take the revenge.
 5. They also drew inspiration from outside India, whereby a very small Japan defeated imperialist Russia.

Bengal:

 * Anushilan Samiti:
   * Initially it was established as a body building society were youth were also exchanging ideas of nationalism.
   * After the partition of bengal it acquired revolutionary character and within a short span of time 3 branches of it were was created i.e. Midnapore (led by Gyanendra nath Bas), Calcutta (Barindra Ghosh {brother of Aurobindo Ghosh}, Bhupendra nath Dutta {brother of Swami Vivekananda}) and Dhaka (Pulin Bihari Das).
   * The objective of Anushilan Samiti was to kill unpopular British officers, informers and traitors.
   * They also conducted Swadeshi Dacoities to raise funds for purchasing arms and to initiate an armed fight with British.
   * Activities of Anushilan Samiti:
     * Bright youth members of Anushilan were sent to Japan and Germany to learn the art of bomb making (Hemant Kanungo and Ullaskar Datta {💌 story}).
     * They created a bomb making factory at Maniktalla, calcutta.
     * Two revolutionaries Prafulla Chaki and Khudiram Bose tried to kill hated police commissioner of calcutta but due to some miscalculation they killed two british ladies. Out of guilt Prafulla chaki shot himself dead and later Khudiram Bose was arrested and executed.
     * British authority issued shoot at sight warrant against revolutionaries which led to arrest of large number of revolutionaries like Brindra Ghosh, Bhupendra nath Datta, Ullaskar Dutta etc.
     * These revolutionaries were handed death punishment but due to intervention of chittranjan das. Death punishment was converted into life imprisonment at cellular jail in Andaman.
     * This case was Faught in Alipore Calcutta therefore c/as Alipore conspiracy case.
     * Aurobindo was also framed in this case but he was successfully defended by chittranjan das. After getting exonerated from judicial process Aurobindo decided quit active politics and he embarked on spiritual journey towards Pondicherry.

Delhi:

 * Rash Bihari Bose and Sachindranath Sanyal:
   * In 1912, They decided to kill the viceroy of India Hardinge.
   * When viceroy was taking elephant ride he was attack but it did not led to his killing. Viceroy was critically injured and this led to framing of Delhi Conspiracy Case.
   * It further led to arrest of 4 revolutionaries Awadh Bihari, Amir Chand, Blamukund, and Basanth who were executed and Sachindranath Sanyal was sent to cellular Jail Andaman.

Madras:

 * Vanchi Iyer:
   * He killed hated Tax collector of Tirunelveli {Robert Ashe}

Bombay:

 * Abhinav Bharat:
   * It was created by two brothers Vinayak Damodar Savarkar and Ganesh Damodar Savarkar in 1904.
   * Earlier in 1899, VS created “Nasik Mitra Mela” through he promoted nationalism.
   * In 1906 he got opportunity to study law from England.
   * In Britain, he revolutionary group of India House and wrote a text “Mazzini Charitra” based on life of Italian revolutionary Mazzini. Within the first month, more than 2000 copies of this book were sold.
   * His popularity started to increase in Britain. OTOH in India, members of Abhinav Bharat, Anant Lakshaman Kanhare and Vinayak Deshpande assassinated Nasik Magistrate AMT Jackson.
   * Meanwhile in Britain, one of the revolutionary from India House assassinated Curzon Wylie in London in 1909.
   * All these incidents led to arrest of revolutionaries in Britain and India. Majority of them were deported to cellular jail in Andaman. Which also included deportation of VDS for 2 life term.

[api/attachments/WvcxateXz2Vf/image/image.png]

Outside India:

 * Bharat Swadeshi Swashasan Samiti India House:
   * It was created by Shayamji Krishna Verma in London. His organization supported indian students who were studying in Britain.
   * He also provided residential accommodation to the indian students and inspired them from nationalist ideas.
   * He also released a revolutionary magazine “Indian sociologist”.
   * VDS became part of India house during his stay in Britain.
 * Madam Bhikaji Cama:
   * She was a rich Parsi lady married to Rustam Cama, who was a pro british lawyer and wanted to enter into politics with the british support.
   * She was a nationalist and during the spread of Bubonic plague in Bombay, she joined the team of Grant Medical college to care for the afflicted. but she also got afflicted with plague.
   * For better recovery she was sent to Britain. After her recuperation initially she came in contact with DBN and later with Shayamji Krishna Verma.
   * She became an active member of India house when british authority were pondering over to deport her to India, she shifted to Paris where she created Paris indian society with the help of SR Rana and MB Godrej in 1906.
   * She happened to be the 1st indian who designed the indian flag. This flag has 3 color strip i.e. Green, Yellow, and Saffron. On the green part 8 lotus flowers were created which represented 8 british indian provinces. In middle strip in Devanagari script “Vande Mataram” was written and in the lower strip image of sun and moon represented indian secularism. for the 1st time this flag was unfurled at International Socialist conference at Stuttgart 1907.
   * [api/attachments/RmIz3yAhk8ia/image/image.png]
 * Indian Nationalism activity in North America:
   * Reasons:
     1. A large number of indian who were living in north America felt there is more freedom and liberty in NA as compared to India.
     2. They came in touch with the American values of liberty, equality, rationality which was enshrined in the American Constitution.
     3. British colonizers present in NA racially differentiated with indians.
     4. Indians also got contaminated with communist ideas and ideologies in America.
     5. There was enhanced freedom to create organization and release Newspapers and magazines. As a result following activities were happened.
   * Swadesh Sevak at Vancouver by GD Kumar
   * United India House at Seattle By Taraknath and GD Kumar.
     * Taraknath released a magazine called “Free Hindustan”
   * Ramnath Puri released a circular k/as “Circular-i-Azadi”
   * The most important organization was “Hindi Association of Portland”
 * Hindi Association of Portland
   * the 1st meeting of Hindi association was held at house of Kanshiram and it was attended by Bhai Parmanand, Soham Singh Bhakna, Harnam Singh Tundilat, Lala Hardayal.
   * In this meeting they decided to create a “Jugantar Aashram” at San Francisco and further decided to release a bi-weekly newspaper “The Ghadar”.
   * Lala Haryadal was the editor of this newspaper. In one of the issue he re-released Yugantar circular appreciating attack on Viceroy Hardinge.
   * In another edition he wrote a very famous and sensational article “Angrezi Raaj ka Kachha Chhitha” in which he ripped apart the exploitative phase of british authority and called for an armed rebellion against british authority.
 * The Ghadar Movement
   * Immediate factor: Komagata Maru incident
     * In 1913, british authority allowed that some indians can visit Canada as such a Japanese ship Komagatamaru was commissioned for the purpose.
     * Large number of indians boarded the ship and started for Canada. once they reached to coast of Vancouver Canadian authorities did not allowed them to disembark form the ship.
     * after few days they were forced to return back to India. Reaching the coast of Budge-Budge near Calcutta, indians clashed with british officials as such british open fire which led to killing of more than 50 ppl and majority of them were form Punjab.
     * It sparked the initiation of Ghadar Movement form North western India which rapidly engulfed entire north India.
   * Developments during Ghadar movement:
     * in north and north-west it was organized by Rash Bihari Bose and it also led to foundation of Ghadar party, whose president was Sohan Singh Bhakna.
     * They tried to enlist the help of German chancellor Zimmerman to supply weapons to indian revolutionaries.
     * They tried to provoke indian army to revolt against british authority and participate in Ghadar movement.
     * A young boy named Kartar Singh Sarabha was executed by british authorities which led to very high anti-British emotion.
     * Ghadarites maintained that the movement must be completely secular and there is no scope of religious fanaticism and polarization.
     * Despite brave efforts it was completely suppressed by 1915. Though it continued in the other parts of the world e.g. in San Francisco till 1917.
   * Reasons for its failure: There were many flaws in the Ghadar movement which are as follows
     1. their ideology of violent overthrow of british form india w/o having a standing army of their own.
     2. They were dependent on arms and armaments from germany.
     3. They had wishful thinking that indian army will join the revolt.
     4. They overestimated their strength and under estimated the strength of british.
     5. Initial strategy created by Lala Hardayal failed completely.
     6. They also faced financial problems to continue with the revolt.
   * Significance of the revolt.
     * It united indians who are living in abroad and infused them with the sense of nationalism.
     * One of the significant contribution of this movement was theme of India's freedom form british colonial rule became an important topic of debate and discussion on the international forums.
     * It created a new genre of revolutionaries who are going to play a very important role in the 3rd of 20th century to carry forward legacy of armed struggle against british authority.
     * They promoted socialist, secular, democratic ideals during the course of the movement.

title: Struggle Continues !!
content:
# EARLY PHASE OF GANDHI IN SOUTH AFRICA:

 * G studied law from Britain when he returned back to India, he was unemployed for many months.
 * Finally in 1893, he got opportunity to fight a case in SA.
 * He went SA to sort out legal problems of his client Dada Abdulla (Gujarati Merchant).
 * During his stay he observed 3 types of indians
   * Indentured laborer's from south India who are working in south African mines.
   * Gujarati Muslim Memon merchants
   * Their children's who were born in SA but did not enjoyed any privileges.
 * They were not able to oppose british authority due to their non efficiency in English language. Moreover they accepted slavery as a way of life.
 * Gandhi offered them to teach English and make them efficient in English language.
 * He himself faced racial discrimination in SA. During his one of travels Pretovia → Johnsburg, he purchased 1st class 
   ticket but on the complain of white man he was bundled out form train. He was forced to travel in drivers box. Once he reached Johannesburg he started searching for room and he did not got any as such he has to spend his night on railway station.
 * When Gandhi solved the problems of dada Abdulla, he decided to go back to India. At this time Indians requested to Gandhi to stay a little more and solve the problem of indians which is basically the issue of dis-enfranchise Indians
 * Gandhi decide to stay a little more but his stay went on to linger for 20 years.

Passive Resistance phase of Gandhi (1894-1906):

 * In 1894, he founded Natal Indian Congress, to fight against the oppressive measures adopted by british authority against indians.
 * Initially he adopted moderate methods like prayers, petitions, sending request to South African legislature and colonial secretary in London.
 * in 1899, he created ambulance corps of indians during 2nd Boer War. He provided medical support to b's who were injured or afflicted in this war.
 * Impressed by services of Gandhi british government conferred upon him title of Kaiser-i-hind.
 * In 1903, Gandhi released his 1st magazine on Indians “Indian Opinion” in which he regularly highlighted the atrocities on indians.
 * Once again in 1906, he created Ambulance cops of indian during Zulu war but this time no favor was returned by british authority.

Satyagraha Phase (1906-1914):

A Satyagrahi is a person who will never bow down before any evil or unjust law. He will remain fearless, courageous but he will not resort to violence.

The main issues of this time were:

 * It was compulsory for indians to carry the registration certificates all the time.
 * A restriction was imposed on their movement they were not allowed to move out from Natal Province.
 * There was indirect promotion of Christianity and those marriages which were not conducted according to Christian methods were invalidated. At this time Gandhi suggested indians to cross Natal province and enter into Transvaal

Inspired by John Ruskin's book “Unto this Last”, Gandhi created Phoenix farm which is considered as birthplace of satyagraha. It was also the place where satyagrahi's were trained.

He was also highly inspired form Leo Tolstoy's book “Kingdom of God is within you”. To provide alternative way of living he founded “Tolstoy Farm” with his German friend Kallenbach.

Finally in 1910-1914, round of negotiations happened b/n Gandhi, General Smut, Viceroy Hardinge and CF Andrews. Which resulted into acceptance of majority of the Gandhi's demand.

A satisfied Gandhi started his journey back to India and landed on 9th January 1915. This day is celebrated as Pravasi Bhartiya Diwas in current times. {It was started on 9th January 2003}.

# HOME RULE MOVEMENT

In 1914, tilak was released form Jail and he wanted to restart his political activity in India. This time he was very cautious and appealed to indians to cooperate with british authority during WW1. Ms. Annie Besant also wanted to initiate a political movement against british rule in India.

Home Rule movement was indian response to WW1 but in much organized manner than the Ghadar movement. During WW1 there was requisitioning of food supplies particularly wheat, rice, pulses. It led to scarcity of food grains, demand supply gap, high inflation in food prices, and life living cost increased.

In this scenario, Tilak and Annie Besant wanted to bring back accountability of British authority through Home rule movement.

Tilak's League:

 * Tilaks league became operational in April 1916, in the areas of Central Province, MH, KN, Bidar, Berar. It had total 6 branches and it was highly organized.
 * Tilak's league had 3 demand:
   1. Swaraj (Self-rule)
   2. Creation of linguistic states
   3. Education in vernacular medium

Annie Besant's League:

 * It became operational in Sept 1916 and it was operational in rest of India including Bombay. It had more than 200 branches but as compared to Tilak's league it was loosely organized.
 * With league of Annie Besant large number of indian nationalist got associated like Motilal Nehru, Jawaharlal Nehru, MMM, MAJ, Bipin Chandra Pal, Tej Bahadur Sapru, Bholabhai Desai, Gaurishankar Mishra, Kedarnath Pandey etc.
 * George Arundale was the secretary of this league and CP Wadia was important officer.

Objectives & Methods:

 * They wanted to create political awareness in society and spread a political education so that people can become assertive about their rights.
 * They adopted unique methods of creating awareness like Public Speeches, organizing libraries, reading rooms, propaganda through posters and pamphlets, and also dramatic depiction through street plays.

Response towards HRM:

 * A case was filed against Tilak but this time he was successfully defended by MAJ.
 * British came very hard on Annie Besant. They arrested so as Wadia and Arundale.
 * After the arrest of Annie Besant there was huge protest and movement all over India demanding release of Annie Besant.
 * At this time SoS Montague observed and commented “Shiva cut his wife into 52 pieces only to discover that he had 52 wives” This is what happens to GoI when it interns Ms. Annie Besant.
 * There was no option other than releasing Annie Besant form Jail.
 * At the height of her popularity, on the suggestion of Tilak she became 1st women president of congress in 1917.

Fading out of Home Rule Movement:

 * in 1918, SoS Montague announced future reforms that will come in 1919, Annie Besant accepted it and called off her movement.
 * A british journalist, Valentine Chirole claimed that Tilak was “Father of Indian Unrest”. Tilak went to Britain to file a defamation case against him. He was away for several months form India and when he returned back he was terminally ill as such his HRM became non functional.

[api/attachments/bniKIOBqXOak/image/image.png]

Significance of HRM:

 1. It created a large group of ardent nationalist who played a vital role in the upcoming decades.
 2. It created organizational link between the nationalist leaders in the urban space and masses in the ruler areas.
 3. The organizational framework created by HRM leaguers was to be utilized later by Gandhi in his movements
 4. It increased the reach of print media and journalism even to the countryside of India.

Annual Session of INC Lucknow (1916):

 * It was presided by Ambika Charan mazumdar. This session led to unification of Moderates and Extremists congress.
 * Moreover for the 1st time Muslim League participated in the annual session of congress, which led to historic Lucknow pact b/n Congress and ML.
 * Congress accepted separate electorate for Muslim masses and INC also agreed that Muslims must be given 1/3rd representation in central and provincial legislative council.
 * Analysis of Lucknow Pact:
   * (+) It allayed the fear of minority to be dominated by majority and for shorter period of time. It bridged the gap b/n Hindu and Muslim masses.
   * (-) INC was unable to understand that approval to separate electorate may create domino effect in the society where by ppl of other religion, caste, and community will also demand separate electorate and then congress will have no ground to reject it. It may lead to Balkanization of India.

# EARLY PHASE OF GANDHI IN INDIA:

Regional interventions:

Gandhi returned back to India on 9th January 1915 form South Africa. In his initial years he did not participated in any national movement.

On the question of HRM he said he would join any organization to affect its policy and not to be affected by it. (Thus he did not participated in HR agitation). Gandhi decided to tour all over India to understand grievances of people of India.

American thinker “Henry David Thoreau” was the progenitor of idea of civil disobedience. But Gandhi was 1st to implement this.

After his initial ventures he decided to address some of the regional problems which are as follows:

 1. Champaran Satyagraha 1917:
    * European planters involved cultivators of Champaran into an agreement k/as Tinkathia System. As per this agreement cultivators were forced to grow indigo on 3/20th of their land holding.
    * In 1890s German synthetic blue dye were invented and it completely forced out indigo form the market.
    * Now it was necessary for European planters to release cultivators from obligation of growing indigo but they turned necessity into opportunity. They started demanding illegal release price form the cultivators.
    * At this point of time a local leader Rajkumar Shukla persuaded Gandhi to intervene. As such Gandhi decided to visit Champaran. Once Gandhi was there, he was forbidden by district magistrate,
    * He disobeyed him and entered into Champaran to start his investigation.{1st example of civil disobedience}
    
    * Gandhi got the support of many of the regional leaders of Bihar which includes names like Rajendra Prasad, Narhari Parikh, JB Kriplani, Mazhar-ul-Haq, and Mahadev Desai {greatest follower of Gandhi and his secretary}.
      
      [api/attachments/60s1lbVGxHeO/image/image.png]
    * Gandhi's investigation created pressure on british authority and they passed an order where by European planters were instructed to stop taking illegal money and to return at least 25% of illegal money that they have taken from cultivators.
 2. Ahmedabad Mill Strike 1918:
    * Problem at Ahmedabad was discontinuation of Plague bonus and non-increment in the wages of workers in the very long time.
    * At this time workers persuaded Gandhi to intervene.
    * Gandhi met one of the mill owners, Ambalal Sarabhai and he tried to convince him to submit entire issue to a tribunal.
    * initially Ambalal agreed for it but later other mill owners refused. At this time Gandhi decided to go on hunger strike to create pressure on mill owners.{1st example of hunger strike}
    * It created pressure on mill owners and they agreed to submit the entire issue to the tribunal.
    * Tribunal gave its judgement whereby it said 35% increase in the wages is justified.
    * {in this issue one the biggest supporter of Gandhi was Anusuya Behan who was the sister of Ambalal Sarabhai}
 3. Kheda Satyagraha 1919:
    * Problem at Kheda was crop failure due to which peasants demanded remission of land revenues.
    * Earlier, GKGs Servants Society of India conducted a survey in Kheda and justified demand of peasants.
    * At this time Gandhi was member of Gujarat Sabha and he played a vital role in this agitation. He was supported by the popular regional leaders of Kheda i.e. Sardar Patel, Vitthalbhai Patel, and Indulal Yagnik.
    * Gandhi advice to peasants to withhold the revenue and fight with evil system till their last breath.
    * Gandhi's appeal created an electrifying effect and ppl stopped paying taxes. It also brought embarrassment to british authority. 1st they suspended collection of revenue and also passed an instruction those who can afford to pay can pay the revenue.

GoI Act 1919 / Montague-Chelmsford Reforms:

 1. It introduced Dyarchy in the provinces, which means two authority i.e. popular ministers and executive councilors.
 2. It created two type of subjects i.e.
    1. Reserved Subjects → were kept under the administration of GG and his ex council. It included subjects like finance, law order, land revenue and irrigation.
    2. Transferred Subjects → were under popular ministers and it included subjects like education, Agriculture, health, industry, and local govt.
 3. It created 3 list of legislation i.e. Federal, Provincial and Concurrent.
 4. It introduced Bicameral legislation at center.
 5. It maintained that women can be given voting rights conditional to the discretion of provincial governmets.
 6. In case of failure of constitutional machinery in the provinces, administration of transferred subjects was to be done by executive councilors under the leadership of governor.
 7. It extended separate electorate for Sikhs, Anglo-Indian and India Christians.

# ROWLETT BILL AND ROWLETT SATYAGRAHA

This bill was named after Sydney Rowlett who was the chairman of committee of Bill. It was aimed at curtailing Civil liberties of indians in the name of curbing terrorist violence. The timing of bill was very wrong so as the intention.

It was the time when indians were expecting more political concessions and privileges british authority disappointed indian leadership.

At this time Gandhi suggested to initiate all India satyagraha. This movement will include protest, mass hartal, procession, fasting and prayers.

The official date of movement was 6th April 1919 but in north indian particularly in Delhi, Ambala, Jalandhar, Amritsar and Lahore it started little early which also led to violence on streets.

As a result B's arrested two popular leaders from Amritsar Dr. Satyapal and Saifuddin Kitchlew.

Jallianwala Bagh Massacre:

On 13th April 1919, Baisakhi day large number of ppl gathered in Amritsar to celebrate the festivity. A good number of people started attending a public speech at an open ground of Jallianwala. It is the moment when commanding officer of Amritsar General Dyre ordered his regiment to fire upon unarmed croud until their bullets are exhausted.

The official estimate of govt was 379 dead but real count was more than 1000 dead.

Entire country was stunned and shocked with the brutality with the british.

 * RNT returned the title of knighthood and Gandhi returned title of Kaiser-i-Hind.
 * Deenbandhu CF Andrews went on to say “This was the most heinous crime against humanity that he has ever seen”.
 * British government appointed Hunter commission to look into the brutality byt it gave a very lack-luster report. It did not prescribe any punishment to General Dyre. And Lt. Governor of Punjab Michael o Dyre. It just gave a soft warning to them
 * Meanwhile in Britain General Dyre was celebrated as hero. In house of lords he was hailed as a Lion who saved british empire in India.
 * Moreover the Morning Post newspaper organization collected 30000 pound for General Dyre.

Later the revenge of this killing was taken by Udham Singh, who assassinated Michael o Dyre in 1940.

[api/attachments/CedyBqe7ZnJh/image/image.png]

# KHILAFAT ISSUE AND NCM:

In the WW1, indian muslim sepoys were given assurance by british authority that after the end of war they will not disturb the position of Khalifa (Sultan of Turkey was considered as spiritual leader of Islam and he was given that title).

They also assured that authority of Khalifa on sacred religious places of Islam will remain intact.

After the end of war, B's broke their promise and removed Khalifa from the position of sultan form Turkey.

At this time in Indian Khilafat committee was constituted under the leadership of Md. Ali, Shaukat Ali, Hasrat Mohani, Abul Kalam Azad, Ajmal Kham.

They demanded:

 1. B's must restore position of Khalifa.
 2. After territorial rearrangement, Khalifa must be left with enough territory so that he can exercise control over holy religious places of Islam.

Later Gandhi also became member of khilafat committee and he also went on to become president of committee. he advocated for khilafat non cooperation movement to address the issue of:

 1. Indian Muslims
 2. and rectification of Punjab wrongs.

Moreover there was very high dis-satisfaction with GoI Act 1919.

Special Session of INC 1920 (Calcutta):

 * It was presided by LLR {the 1st president from extremist side}.
 * Gandhi proposed his idea of NC and went on to claim that if NC is initiated then indian will get its freedom within 1 year.
 * Gandhi was vehemently opposed by CR Das. Moreover LLR also did not agreed with Gandhi.
 * Though majority of the people supported Gandhi's idea of NC but final decision was left for annual session to be held at Nagpur.

Annual Session of INC 1920 (Nagpur):

 * It was presided by Vijay Raghava Cheriyar. In this session NC related proposals were moved by CR Das and Gandhi made proposals related to Congress organization.
 * NCM related proposals:
   * Boycott of foreign goods and services, foreign titles, british administered educational institutes, courts, visits of High officials and monarchs from Britain
   * Promotion and to spun khadi, distribution of charkha.
   * Boycott of central and provincial legislative councils.
 * Congress related proposals:
   * Gandhi proposed the things related to bringing more effectiveness in the functioning of congress organization
     1. creation of CWC to look into day to day affairs of congress.
     2. creation of provincial congress committees on linguistic basis.
     3. creation of all India congress committee to coordinate b/n provincial committees.
     4. As extent possible create congress committees in villages and towns.
     5. Gandhi further recommended the annual subscription of congress or membership fee must be reduced to 25 paise so as congress reaches to even countryside of India.
     6. As extent possible develop Hindi written in Devanagari script as a contact language in provinces.

Development during NCM:

 1. Boycott of foreign goods, services, titles, educational institutes and courts was very successful.
 2. People like Rajendra Prasad, C Rajagopalachari, and Sardar Patel left the practice of law for sometime.
 3. Khadi and charkha started to emerge as National symbol.
 4. In 1921, Tilak Swaraj Fund was created and within a short span of time, 1cr rupees was collected in the fund.
 5. Women enthusiastically contributed to this fund by giving their jewelry. Even many courtesan's contributed to swaraj fund.
 6. In this duration there was foundation of 4 prominent educational institutes i.e. Kashi Vidyapeeth, Bihar Vidyapeeth, Gujarat Vidyapeeth, and Jamia Millia Islamia.
 7. Meanwhile MMM facilitated talks b/n Gandhi and viceroy Reading. But talks failed.
 8. In 1922, Prince of Wales arrived in India which led to huge protest and movement in bombay and also violence on the streets of Bombay. Gandhi observed one week fast to control the violence.
 9. Gandhi also gave ultimatum to british authority that if political prisoners are not released within a week, then he will initiate civil disobedience from Bardoli.

Chauri-Chaura Incident:

 * A retired british indian army soldier Bhagwan Ahir initiated a protest form Gauri Bazaar against high inflation in food.
 * On 4th Feb 1922, he was joined by 2500 protesters. Police officials opened fire which led to killing of 3 ppl.
 * As such masses retaliated and they torched the police station. which led to killing of 22 police men. All of them were indians.

After chauri Chaura incident, Gandhi decided to call off NCM from Bardoli Resolution 12th Feb. British authority arrested Gandhi on the charges of sedition and he was sent to jail for 6 years.

At this time Gandhi was criticized for calling off NCM when it was going good. Moreover some nationalist went on to say that why entire nation will pay the cost of violence to few

Gandhi replied that if he does not condemns the violence of chauri Chaura incident then it will be an inspiration or provocation to others to indulge in violence.

At this time Gandhi favored the strategy of Struggle-Truce-Struggle (STS). He was of the view that masses cannot sacrifice endlessly and after an intense period of struggle there is a need of period of truce in which masses can rejuvenate and reprepare for the next bigger phase of struggle.

Analysis of NCM:

 * NCM made congress as party of masses and it also improved reach of congress to the hinterland of India.
 * It created connecting link b/n the masses and the nationalist in the rural areas.
 * Congress realized the true potential of India lies with masses in the rural areas and effectiveness of any mass movement depends on their participation.
 * For a shorter period of time there was complete cooperation b/n H's and M's.
 * From NCM huge participation of women in national movement started. {because of the nature of protest was changed → Non violence → they can participate w/o loss of dignity and can contribute equally as that of men}

Criticism of NCM:

 * Great participation of educated middle class Muslims in NCM was on the basis of anti-imperial religious consciousness. But congress as an organization failed to convert this consciousness into politico-secular consciousness.

By the end of 1923, Khilafat issue also lost its relevance. People of turkey rose against Khalifa and under the leadership of Mustafa Kamal Pasha they removed sultan of turkey or Khalifa for his position and it emerged as secular democratic state.

# PHASE OF SWARAJIST (1920 -1926):

After Gandhi's arrest a sense of confusion started to prevail in congress. Leaders like CRD and MLN wanted the change in strategy of congress.

In 1922, annual session of congress held at Gaya and it was presided by CRD. In this session he proposed to end the boycott of councils and enter into provincial and legislative councils.

He was backed by MLN and they came to be known as Pro-changers. They further said they are not giving up hope on NCM in fact once they enter into councils they will extend NCM into councils by hindering each and every work of council. {end or mend}

OTOH, Leaders like SVP, C Rajagopalachari, Rajendra Prasad opposed council entry and advice the constructive work suggested by Gandhi.

 1. Upliftment of backwards and women's
 2. Build H-M unity
 3. Removal of UTy.

Since they oppose council entry they came to be known as no-changers. As a result on 1st Jan 1923 CRD and MLN declared creation of Khilafat Swaraj Party with CRD as president and MLN as secretary after some point of time both the factions realized that their differences will cause harm to national interest {also feared a 1907 like split} and started to find common grounds of cooperation which is as follows:

 * However important council work may be, it cannot replace the mass movement
 * There was no immediate environment of mass movement.
 * Swarajist / Prochangers can be given a chance to enter into the councils till the environment for mass movement is created.

Gandhi's stand on council entry: {→expressed in Belgaum Session 1924 where he was president}

 * He was released from jail in Feb. 1924.
 * He was completely against council entry and believed that it must not have happened at the very first place but he did not opposed council entry in 1924 because it has already happened.
 * and he felt opposing at this time will be seen as weakness of congress organization and it will also bring embarrassment to the nationalist.

Achievements of Swarajist:

 * In the elections 1923, Swarajist performed very well in province of Bombay, Bengal, central province and united province
 * they won 38 out of 101 seats in Central Assembly. {overall 41 as included nominated}
 * While being in council they worked in many fronts like:
   * They advocated more freedom of speech and expression.
   * Development of indigenous industries.
   * To convene a round table conference for the purpose of reviewing GoI Act 1919 and to go ahead with future constitutional reforms
   * They defeated Public Safety bill which was aimed at curbing the activities of communist and socialist leaders.

Gandhi was not very much satisfied with the council entry work. When british PM Stanley Baldwin declared the formation of Simon Commission, Swarajist merged with main congress to work in the direction of mass movement.

In 1925, Swarajist were successful in electing the Vithalbhai Patel as the 1st indian speaker of central legislative council.

Responsivist:

 * Leaders like LLR, MMM, MR Jayakar decided that they will not resign form the Assembly and they will work the reforms in whatever capacity they have been given in the councils.

Qn: The women's questions arose in modern India as a part of 19th century social reform movement. What were the major issue's and debates concerning women in the period?

title: Towards end...
content:
# SIMON COMMISSION:

 * In general there was provision of review of indian constitutional scenario after every 10 years. But initially Lee commission recommended early review of GOI Act 1919.
 * Moreover in 1924, Alexander Muddiman committee recommended creation of India Statutory commission to review the constitutional scenario in India.
 * As per recommendation, ISC was constituted under the leadership of John Simon. It was an all white commission, not even a single indian the most moderate the most liberal was accommodated in the commission which was going to decide the constitutional fate of indians.
 * It was embarrassing for the entire indian class.
 * At this time, liberal federation of Tej Bahadur Sapru proposed Simon Boycott which was approved INC.
 * Hindu Mahasabha and ML also supported Simon boycott (Though there was a small division in ML under the leadership of Md. Shafi who was against Simon boycott but MAJ carried majority in the support of boycott)
 * It was INC which made Simon boycott as a popular movement.
 * The form of boycott included:
   * Protests
   * Processions
   * Black flag demonstration
   * with an exhortation “Simon go back!”

Government Response to boycott:

 * It was lathi charge on the protesting crowd.
 * One of the bad incident related to it happened in Lucknow where GB Pant and JLN were thoroughly beaten. {Pant got paralyzed protecting JLN}
 * But the worst incident happened at Lahore where LLR was hit on chest and on 17th Nov he succumbed to the injuries.
 * After his death youth revolutionaries decided to take the revenge of his killing.
 * [api/attachments/aS2IubLdkTpM/image/image%20(2).jpg]

Gathering Storm (1927-1930):

 * In 1927, there was meeting of all India congress committee in Delhi.
 * In this meeting JLN proposed a snap resolution that congress must aim for Poorna Swaraj rather than Self rule.
 * Majority of the people agreed to this resolution. OTOH, huge protest against Simon commission started. Looking at the protest of indians then SoS Birkenhead challenged indians to come up their own constitutional scheme but make sure that all the major parties and communities are giving their consent to it.
 * Challenge was accepted and a committee was constituted under the leadership of MLN.

Nehru Report (Aug 1928):

 1. Dominion status objective for India.
 2. Responsible government in center and states.
 3. It objected separate electorate for muslim masses. and recommended that muslim masses can be given reservation in those areas where they are minority but not in numerical majority.
 4. Equal right to women including RtV.
 5. Administration of India must be completely secular in orientation.

Objection to Nehru Report:

 * There was vehement opposition of Dominion status provision from the side of JLN, SCB and Satyamurthy. They demanded Poorna Swaraj and went on to create India Independence League.
 * Hindu Mahasabha objected on the provision of secular state.
 * ML rejected the provision of Separate electorate so as Sikhs and Anglo-Indians.

At this time congress maintained that they are giving 1 year space to british authority to implement dominion status but if they are not implementing it then we will aim for Poorna Swaraj.

Meanwhile, news related to conduct of RTC started to float. It was said that all the major parties of India and representatives of all the communities will be invited to Britain to have a equal level discussion with british authority.

Delhi Manifesto:

 * All the major leaders of congress met at Delhi to discuss news related to RTC.
 * They asked british authority that it must be made clear that the purpose of RTC is not to discuss when dominion status would be granted but to formulate the scheme for the immediate implementation of DS.
 * To this Qn, B's did not gave any reply as such the general consensus in congress was not to participate in RTC.

Annual Session of INC 1929 - (Lahore):

 * It was presided by JLN. In this session Poorna Swaraj declaration was made as the objective of congress.
 * It was also decided that congress will not participate in 1st RTC.
 * Moreover, Gandhi was given full authority to initiate CDM.
 * As a mark of symbol of Poorna Swaraj declaration, tricolor flag was unfurled on the banks of river Ravi on 26th Jan 1930. Moreover on this day independence pledge was read out and it was collectively affirmed.

2ND PHASE OF REVOLUTIONARY NATIONALISM (1924-34):

 1. Hindustan Republican Army:
    * It was founded by Sachindranath Sanyal and Yogesh Chatterjee.
    * The objective of this organization was not only to kill unpopular british officers but also to loot british treasury and raise funds for purchasing weapons.
    * Their very 1st activity was looting a train stationed at Kakori in Lucknow.
    * B's filed Kakori Conspiracy Case against revolutionaries.
    * 4 responsible revolutionaries were arrested and immediately executed i.e. Rajendra Lhiri, Roshan Lal, Ramprasad Bismil, Asfaq-ullah-Khan.
 2. Hindustan Socialist Republican Association:
    * In 1928, Chandrashekhar Azad announced the formation of HSRA in Firoz shah Kotla, Delhi.
    * The objective of this organization became establishment of socialism in India. They also decided to abandon the policy of killing of unpopular british officers, informers and traitors.
    * The important members of this organization were Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, Sukhdev, Jatin das, Batukeshwar Dutta.
    * Bhagat Singh:
      * He was born in the family of nationalist on 28th Sept 1907 in Lahore.
      * His uncle Ajit Singh was one of the famous nationalist of the time and he was a close friend of LLR.
      * From his very childhood he had nationalist orientation. He was a voracious reader and highly influenced with the thought process of Karl Marx.
      * During his school days (DAV) he created Lahore Cchatra Sangha to promote nationalism.
      * He did not believed in singular heroics and further said that there cannot be any replacement of mass movement.
      * He hated communal politics and communalism. He did not believed in religion and in this regard he wrote a text “Why i am an Atheist”
      * He went on to say “Political freedom is not the real freedom and that real freedom includes end of all exploitation i.e. external and internal”
    * After the death of LLR, Bhagat Singh, shivaram rajguru and Sukhdev decided to take revenge of his killing and they killed one of the responsible officers John Saunders. After this incident british government issued shoot at sight order.
    * In another instance, Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutta threw a bomb in central assembly when public safety bill was getting passed. It was a low intensity bomb not intended to kill anyone but to make the deaf hear the sound.
    * They did not escaped from the place and quoted their arrest. After their arrest their trial started one of the revolutionary Jatin Das demanded treatment like political prisoners and went on in to an epic fast. It is said that on the 63rd day of his fast he succumbed.
    * OTOH, capital punishment was prescribed to bhagat Singh, R and S. On 23rd march 1931 they were executed.
 3. In Bengal:
    1. Gopinath Saha:
       * He tried to kill the hated police commissioner Charles Tegart in january 1924.
       * But due to some miscalculation he killed somebody else as a result he was over powered and executed.
    2. On 8th December 1930, Benoy Krishna Basu, Badal Gupta and Dinesh Gupta entered into police HQ at writers building at Calcutta.
       * They were dressed like European officer and they intended to kill the head of prison guard NS Simpson.
       * Though they were successful in their attempt but they have to loose their lives. Benoy and Dinesh shot themselves with their own revolvers and Badal Gupta took potassium cyanide.
    3. Two young girls Shanti Ghosh and Suniti Chaudhary successfully killed the hated district magistrate of Comilla, Charles Stevens. {interested in womens so took advantage of him}
    4. Another women revolutionary Beena Das Lt. Gov of bengal Stanley Jackson during her convocation ceremony. She made 5 fire shots but was unsuccessful in killing LG but she was able to seriously injure him.
    5. Indian Republican Army / Chittagong Group:
       * It was founded by Master Surya Sen. He intended to loot weapons from british armories.
       * He organized a raid in Chittagong armory. He was supported by two young girls Preety Lata Wadedar and Kalpana Dutta.
       * During raid Preety was shot dead. Master Surya Sen and Kalpana were able to escape.
       * After this incident SS started to operate from different different locations in disguised form. Bit unfortunately someone informed his location to B's which led to his arrest and finally he was executed on 12th Jan 1934.
       
       * With his death the 2nd phase of revolutionary nationalism came to stand still.
         
         [api/attachments/hlGwgqeTHsXy/image/image.png]

# CIVIL DISOBEDIENCE MOVEMENT:

 * There was a challenge before Gandhi to choose a particular law which is to be offered to for Civil Disobedience. He picked salt act of 1882.
 * As per this act, indians were not allowed to manufacture salt and also they cannot collect salt form natural salt pans.
 * Indian were forced to purchase salt form british manufacturers and mrp of salt included a very heavy salt tax.
 * Gandhi felt that salt is one commodity with which all the indian households will feel connected and moreover braking the salt law will not involve any violence.

Salt March to Dandi:

 * On 12th March 1930, Gandhi started with 78 delegates from his Sabarmati ashram form Ahmedabad.
 * On 6th April 1930 he reached coast of Dandi and took handful of salt and declared that it is a crime against god and humanity to submit any longer before british authority.
 * It marked the beginning of CDM.

Developments during CDM:

 1. In TN, C Rajagopalachari organized salt march form triconapally to vedaranyam coast to break the law.
 2. In Ke, K. Kelappan organized it form Calicut to Payannur.
 3. In assam a band of satyagrahis started march form Sylhet to Naokhali Coast in Bengal.
 4. Sarojini Naidu along with Imam Sahib and Gandhi's son manilal started the march towards dharsana saltworks to break the salt law, but they were thoroughly beaten.
 5. In NWFP, Frontier Gandhi, Khan Abdul Gafar Khan organized CDM through his effective cadre of “Khudai Khidmadgars” aka Red Shirts.
 6. During Peshawar demonstration, one incensed british officer ordered Gurkha regiment to fire on unarmed crowd but they refused to repeat the mistake of Jallianwala Bagh.
 7. In Assam, govt released Cunningham Circular which advised students not to participate in any national movt o/w they will have to face dire consequences. But students started to participate in national movt in much greater numbers.
 8. In Nagaland, Rani Gadinlieu, revolted against B's. It resulted in her arrest and she was kept in prison till India's independence.

Meanwhile 1st RTC was conducted in Britain w/o participation of congress. As such all the discussions of 1st RTC seemed to be abortive. There was a pressure on viceroy Irwin to bring congress to the table anyhow. At this time, Tej Bahadur Sapru and MR Jayakar facilitated talks b/n Gandhi and Irwin.

Gandhi Irwn Pact:

 1. Lenient treatment to govt servants who have resigned.
 2. Remission of all the illegal fines not yet collected.
 3. return of confiscated land not yet sold to 3rd party.
 4. Restoring freedom of press.
 5. Right to make salt for consumption.
 6. Right to peaceful protest and picketing.
 7. (Not accepted) Congress demanded inquiry into police torture on people during CDM
 8. (Not accepted) Gandhi demanded that capital punishment given to bhagat Singh, rajguru, and sukhdev must be converted into lesser punishment.

Annual Session of INC (March 1931):

 * SVP became president of congress. In this session meaning of Poorna Swaraj was defined for masses. It said “Political freedom must include economic freedom” → influenced by BS and socialism.
 * In the session two resolutions were introduced i.e. on
   * Fundamental Rights
   * National Economic Plan
 * Gandhi was permitted to participate in 2nd RTC on behalf of congress.

2nd RTC (Nov 1931):

 * Gandhi along with Mahadev Desai participated in RTC. Even after powerful speech and advocacy Gandhi was not able to achieve anything.
 * He returned back to India empty handed.
 * During 2nd RTC, Winston Churchill went on to say “Gandhi was half-naked seditious Fakir”. When he returned back to India he faced severe criticism.
 * At this time, gujarati poet Zevarchand Meghani wrote for Gandhi “Even this last cup of poison you must drink Bapu”

Meanwhile in India, Irwin was replaced by new viceroy Willingdon, who was an imperialist to the core. He came to India with 3 policy considerations:

 1. Gandhi must not be allowed to resume CDM
 2. In general no national movement must be permitted to gather force.
 3. To reassure british loyalist that british government has not lost its will to rule in India and it is very much in tact.

When Gandhi resumed CDM, Willingdon came up with civil martial law. Civil liberties no longer existed, people were tortured and arrested according to whims and fancies of britishers.

Within 2 months >80k people were arrested, which was also leading to many violent instances form the different parts of the country. At this time Gandhi started his Harijan Tour to create awareness about upliftment of untouchables.

In Nov 1933, he shifted his ashram form Sabarmati to Wardha for harijan upliftment program. In April 1934, formally Gandhi withdrew CDM.

Communal Award and Poona Pact:

 * On 16th Aug 1932, British PM Ramsay McDonald announced communal award for depressed classes. According to this award, depressed classes were entitled separate electorate and status of minority.
 * Gandhi opposed it vehemently, he said that it is attack on Indian unity and nationalism. British want to break Hinduism.
 * He further added that if depressed classes are to be treated as minority then no body will try to reform untouchability and untouchables will remain untouchables in perpetuity.
 * Gandhi announced his fast unto death against communal award.
 * Poona Pact:
   * Representative of depressed classes, DrA agreed for Poona pact with Gandhi.
   * As per the pact, separate electorate provision was to be abandoned and more seats were to be reserved in central and provincial legislative assembly.

# GOI ACT 1935:

Making / considerations:

 * Recommendation of Simon Commission
 * Deliberations of 3 RTCs
 * White Papers
 * Report of Joint Select Committee
 * recommendations of SoS Samuel Hoare.

Provisions:

 1. It declared to create All India Federation comprising of british indian provinces and princely states. but creation of federation was conditional on the fulfillment of two criteria
    1. Half of the princely states must give their consent to join federation
    2. The aggregate population in joining princely states must be more than ½ of total population of all the princely states {none of the criterial was fulfilled as such, federation never became operational}
 2. Diarchy was removed form the provinces and provincial autonomy was sought to be provided.
 3. At center Diarchy was introduced as per this arrangement GGEC will manage reserved subjects and GGLC will manage transferred subjects.
 4. It created 3 list of legislations i.e. Federal, Provincial, and concurrent.
 5. Bicameral legislation at center was reconstituted with upper house and lower house. Moreover princely states were allocated 104 seats in upper house. Members of princely states were nominated and from british ruled states were elected.
 6. This act led to the establishment of:
    * Federal court of India
    * Federal bank of India
    * Federal PSC
    * Provincial PSC and Joint PSC.
 7. It enfranchised approx. 10 to 14% population of India.

Annual Session of INC 1936 (Lucknow):

 * It was presided by JLN. In this session all the members of congress equivocally rejected GoI Act 1935.

Annual Session of INC 1937 (Faizpur):

 * It was presided by JLN. This was the 50th session of congress and it was meant to discuss the problems of peasants of India.
 * But surprisingly there was no discussion on abolition of zamindari

Provincial elections 1937:

 * Though congress rejected GoI Act 1935 route and branch but all the congress members agreed that congress must fight elections to deepen anti imperial feeling.
 * But congress members differed on the question of → formation of ministries in provinces.
 * Leaders like JLN, SCB, and congress socialist leaders rejected the idea of congress ministries they said creation of ministry will be the negation of rejection of 1935 act.
 * Leaders like Rajagopalachari, MA Ansari and satyamurthi advocated formation of ministries
 * Gandhi's Stand:
   * Initially, he was hesitant on this topic but later after good election performance he was inclined to give a trial to congress ministries.
   * He advised them to “hold the offices lightly not tightly”
 * Congress contested on 760 seats out of 1161 seats in 11 british indian provinces. Congress got clear majority in 6 states i.e. up, cp, bombay, madras, bihar, odisha. Congress formed coalition in 3 states i.e. Sindh, assam, nwfp.
 * In Punjab, unionist party of Sikandar Hayat khan formed the government.
 * In Bengal coalition government of ML and Krishak Praja party formed the government.

Work of Congress ministries:

 * They led by example and tried to adopt austere measures.
 * They controlled communal violence in their respective states and also tried to foster unity b/n H's and M's.
 * Political prisoners not convicted for violence were released form Jail.
 * Freedom of press was restored.
 * Ban on many organization was lifted eg. Hindustan Seva Dal and Youth League. But ban on Communist Party of India remained intact as it was imposed by central government.
